abstract:
We introduce REAL, a benchmark and framework for multi-turn agent evaluations
on deterministic simulations of real-world websites. REAL comprises
high-fidelity, deterministic replicas of 11 widely-used websites across domains
such as e-commerce, travel, communication, and professional networking. We also
release a benchmark consisting of 112 practical tasks that mirror everyday
complex user interactions requiring both accurate information retrieval and
state-changing actions. All interactions occur within this fully controlled
setting, eliminating safety risks and enabling robust, reproducible evaluation
of agent capability and reliability. Our novel evaluation framework combines
programmatic checks of website state for action-based tasks with rubric-guided
LLM-based judgments for information retrieval. The framework supports both
open-source and proprietary agent systems through a flexible evaluation harness
that accommodates black-box commands within browser environments, allowing
research labs to test agentic systems without modification. Our empirical
results show that frontier language models achieve at most a 41% success rate
on REAL, highlighting critical gaps in autonomous web navigation and task
completion capabilities. Our framework supports easy integration of new tasks,
reproducible evaluation, and scalable post-training data generation, marking a
significant step forward in evaluating and advancing agent capabilities.

keyInsights: REAL successfully bridges the gap between realism and reproducibility, enabling controlled experimentation on complex web environments.
The benchmark identifies critical deficiencies in current LLM-powered agents, particularly regarding failure recognition, state verification, and recovery from navigation dead ends.
The deterministic nature and robust reward system of REAL provide a suitable environment for generating training data and applying reinforcement learning to improve web agents.
originalProblem: Existing web agent benchmarks often use simplified interfaces or artificial tasks, failing to reflect real-world complexity and utility.
The inherent non-determinism of live websites makes reproducible evaluation nearly impossible and introduces safety concerns for agent interaction.
Prior environments lack the necessary fidelity, configurability, and clear reward signals required for effectively training and evaluating large language model-powered agents.
results: The best-performing model, Claude 3.7 Sonnet-Thinking, achieved a 41.07% success rate across all tasks, with Gemini 2.5 Pro Experimental at 38.39%.
Open-source models generally lagged, with Llama-4-Maverick achieving 12.50% and OpenAI’s Computer-Using Agent (CUA) scoring only 7.14%.
Analysis revealed common failure modes including agents not verifying actual website state post-action and getting stuck in non-standard navigation flows without recovery strategies.
solution: The REAL framework provides 11 publicly hosted, high-fidelity replicas of consumer websites, designed to be fully deterministic for reproducible experimentation.
It includes 112 realistic, multi-turn tasks across diverse domains, evaluated using a combination of programmatic state verification and LLM-based judging.

The system offers configurable environments via URL parameters to simulate specific scenarios and edge cases, alongside a flexible agent harness supporting various interaction models like Playwright and Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed REAL, a benchmark featuring 11 deterministic, high-fidelity simulations of real-world websites and 112 multi-turn tasks to evaluate autonomous web agents. The evaluation of frontier language models on REAL revealed that no agent achieved higher than a 41.07% success rate, indicating significant limitations in current capabilities.

title: REAL: Benchmarking Autonomous Agents on Deterministic Simulations of  Real Websites

abstract: We introduce Meta Agents Research Environments (ARE), a research platform for scalable creation of environments, integration of synthetic or real applications, and execution of agentic orchestrations. ARE provides simple abstractions to build complex and diverse environments, each with their own rules, tools, content, and verifiers, helping to bridge the gap between model development and real-world deployment. We also propose Gaia2, a benchmark built in ARE and designed to measure general agent capabilities. Beyond search and execution, Gaia2 requires agents to handle ambiguities and noise, adapt to dynamic environments, collaborate with other agents, and operate under temporal constraints. Unlike prior benchmarks, Gaia2 runs asynchronously, surfacing new failure modes that are invisible in static settings. Our experiments show that no system dominates across the intelligence spectrum: stronger reasoning often comes at the cost of efficiency, and budget scaling curves plateau, highlighting the need for new architectures and adaptive compute strategies. Perhaps more importantly, ARE abstractions enable continuous extension of Gaia2 to other environments, empowering the community to rapidly create new benchmarks tailored to their domains. In AI's second half, progress increasingly depends on defining meaningful tasks and robust evaluations to drive frontier capabilities forward.

keyInsights: An 'inverse scaling law' exists for time-sensitive tasks: while stronger reasoning generally improves accuracy, it often correlates with slower responses, highlighting a critical tradeoff between performance, computational cost, and execution speed for practical agents.

Frontier models demonstrate proficiency in instruction-following and basic search but struggle significantly with real-world complexities such as ambiguity resolution, adaptability to dynamic environments, robustness to noise, and effective inter-agent collaboration, indicating current limitations in general agent capabilities.

Multi-agent collaboration, particularly with heterogeneous teams (e.g., powerful main agents with cheaper 'app-agents'), can enhance performance and stability for weaker models and offers a strategy to manage compute-quality tradeoffs for stronger models.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent environments (web-based and simulated) lack the control, diversity, and realism needed for robust model improvement and seamless real-world deployment.
Current benchmarks rely on idealized, sequential agent-environment interactions, omitting crucial real-world elements like asynchronous events, temporal dynamics, and environmental noise.
Scaling LLM training for continuous improvement and 'superintelligence' is hindered by the absence of versatile, verifiable environments that can support complex, long-horizon tasks.
results: GPT-5 (high) achieved the highest overall Pass@1 score on Gaia2 at 42.1%, with other frontier models like Claude 4 Sonnet (34.8%) and Gemini 2.5 Pro (25.8%) trailing, showcasing substantial performance gaps.

Scenario categories focusing on Ambiguity, Adaptability, Time, and Noise proved significantly more challenging for all evaluated models, with most scores falling below 20%, revealing critical weaknesses in handling complex, real-world conditions.

Experiments in 'instant mode' (ablating inference latency) dramatically increased scores on Time scenarios (e.g., GPT-5 (high) jumped from 0% to 34.4%), underscoring that inference speed is as crucial as reasoning capabilities for effective performance in time-sensitive agent applications.
solution: Developed Meta Agents Research Environments (ARE), an event-based, time-driven simulation platform with strict decoupling between the environment and the agent, based on concepts of Apps, Environments, Events, Notifications, and Scenarios.

Proposed Gaia2, a novel benchmark within the ARE framework, comprising 800 unique, verifiable scenarios across 10 Mobile universes to measure general agent capabilities including search, execution, adaptability, time awareness, ambiguity resolution, agent-to-agent collaboration, and noise robustness.

Implemented a rubric-like verifier that checks agent 'write' operations against ground truth for consistency, causality, and timing, leveraging LLM judges for soft checks and ensuring multi-turn validation for accurate reward signals.
paper_summary.summary: Meta's research introduces ARE (Agents Research Environments), a scalable, event-based platform for developing and evaluating LLM agents in dynamic, asynchronous simulations. The accompanying Gaia2 benchmark reveals critical limitations in current frontier LLMs, particularly their performance on tasks requiring adaptability, temporal awareness, and ambiguity resolution, while demonstrating the potential of multi-agent collaboration.

title: ARE: Scaling Up Agent Environments and Evaluations

abstract: As language models (LMs) evolve from chat assistants to long-horizon agents capable of multi-step reasoning and tool use, existing benchmarks remain largely confined to structured or exam-style tasks that fall short of real-world professional demands. To this end, we introduce \$OneMillion-Bench \$OneMillion-Bench, a benchmark of 400 expert-curated tasks spanning Law, Finance, Industry, Healthcare, and Natural Science, built to evaluate agents across economically consequential scenarios. Unlike prior work, the benchmark requires retrieving authoritative sources, resolving conflicting evidence, applying domain-specific rules, and making constraint decisions, where correctness depends as much on the reasoning process as the final answer. We adopt a rubric-based evaluation protocol scoring factual accuracy, logical coherence, practical feasibility, and professional compliance, focused on expert-level problems to ensure meaningful differentiation across agents. Together, \$OneMillion-Bench provides a unified testbed for assessing agentic reliability, professional depth, and practical readiness in domain-intensive scenarios.

keyInsights: The benchmark provides a "value-faithful evaluation" by assigning monetary values to tasks, offering an interpretable and tangible metric for AI's practical utility and economic impact.

Its detailed rubric-based evaluation with a skill-oriented taxonomy (web search, reasoning, verbalization, instruction following) offers enhanced diagnostic power to pinpoint specific agent strengths and weaknesses.

The inclusion of negative penalties for behaviors like factual hallucinations or unsafe generations inherently promotes the development of more trustworthy and responsible AI systems for high-stakes professional contexts.
originalProblem: Existing language agent benchmarks (e.g., HQA, agentic workflow, reality-grounded) often lack the ability to fully simulate the multi-step workflows, evidence reconciliation, decision justification, and economic implications of real professional labor.

There is a recognized "critical rift" between the capabilities demonstrated by evolving language agents and the rigorous demands of real-world professional tasks, where agents must reliably create tangible value.
It remains unclear if language agents can genuinely deliver consistent and quantifiable economic value in high-stakes professional environments, beyond solving isolated problems.
results: CLAUDE-OPUS-4.6 consistently emerged as the top performer across Global and CN subsets, with web search significantly amplifying its advantage, though search capabilities sometimes degraded performance for other models.

Many models exhibited a "near-miss" phenomenon, achieving moderate Expert Scores (e.g., 45-50%) but significantly lower Pass Rates (often below 25%), highlighting a reliability gap in meeting professional competence thresholds.

Performance varied across domains and rubric types, with models scoring highest on "Structure and Formatting" but struggling more with "Factual Information" and "Analytical Reasoning"; all models showed a 15-20% performance drop on temporally sensitive tasks.
solution: The paper introduces $OneMillion-Bench, a benchmark comprising 400 highly challenging open-ended tasks across five professional domains: Finance, Law, Healthcare, Natural Science, and Industry.

Tasks are curated through a three-stage expert-driven pipeline including adversarial validation, peer review, and bidirectional truncation, ensuring practical value, objectivity, and discriminative power.

Agent performance is evaluated using a rubric-based protocol that includes Expert Scores and Pass Rates, features asymmetric weights with negative penalties for professional misconduct, and quantifies economic value for each task based on expert time and wages.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark, $OneMillion-Bench, evaluates language agents on complex, economically consequential professional tasks across five high-stakes domains, revealing a "reliability gap" between current AI agents and human experts. It quantifies the economic value delivered by agents, showing varied impacts of web search and providing detailed diagnostic insights.

title: \$OneMillion-Bench: How Far are Language Agents from Human Experts?

abstract: The potential of Large Language Model (LLM) as agents has been widely acknowledged recently. Thus, there is an urgent need to quantitatively \textit{evaluate LLMs as agents} on challenging tasks in interactive environments. We present AgentBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark that consists of 8 distinct environments to assess LLM-as-Agent's reasoning and decision-making abilities. Our extensive test over \num API-based and open-sourced (OSS) LLMs shows that, while top commercial LLMs present a strong ability of acting as agents in complex environments, there is a significant disparity in performance between them and many OSS competitors that are no larger than 70B. We identify the typical reasons of failures in environments and LLMs, showing that poor long-term reasoning, decision-making, and instruction following abilities are the main obstacles for developing usable LLM agents. Improving instruction following and training on high quality multi-round alignment data could improve agent performance. And different from existing assumptions, training on code present ambivalent impacts on different agent tasks. Datasets, environments, and an integrated evaluation package for AgentBench are released at this https URL.

keyInsights: High-quality alignment data is crucial for developing capable LLM agents, with models like Vicuna-13B achieving competitive scores comparable to larger models due to superior alignment.

Code-tuning exhibits a nuanced effect on agent performance, benefiting static procedural tasks (e.g., Web Shopping) but showing ambivalent or detrimental impacts on tasks requiring general thinking or complex interaction beyond pure code generation (e.g., Digital Card Game).

Robust internal planning and error recovery are essential for effective agency, as top models maintain more coherent thought processes and exhibit self-correction, unlike others that fall into repetitive cycles when encountering errors.
originalProblem: A critical lack of systematic, standardized benchmarks existed for evaluating LLMs in diverse, interactive, and real-world-aligned agent environments.
Prior benchmarks were often confined to single tasks or specific domains, failing to provide a holistic view of an LLM's comprehensive agentic capabilities.
Existing evaluation methods suffered from limitations like modality mismatch for text-only LLMs and the absence of unified, publicly accessible tools for comparative analysis.
results: A significant performance disparity was observed, with top commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-4 achieving an overall AGENTBENCH score of 4.01) substantially outperforming open-source models (average OSS score of 0.51).

"Task Limit Exceeded" was identified as the dominant failure mode, indicating models frequently fail to solve problems within interaction rounds or fall into repetitive generation loops (over 90% of TLE trajectories showed repetition).
GPT-4 led in 6 out of 8 environments, demonstrating strong practical usability in tasks such as House Holding with a 78% success rate, showcasing its advanced agentic capabilities.
solution: Developed AGENTBENCH, a multi-dimensional benchmark comprising 8 distinct, interactive, text-only environments categorized into code-grounded, game-grounded, and web-grounded scenarios.

Formalized the interactive evaluation of LLMs as agents within a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework, employing a 1-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting strategy with greedy decoding for consistent results.
Evaluated 29 diverse LLMs using a unified, scalable Server-Client evaluation toolkit that encapsulates complex task environments in Docker images and supports resumable evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: AGENTBENCH introduces a multi-dimensional benchmark with 8 interactive environments to systematically evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) as agents. The benchmark reveals a significant performance gap between commercial and open-source LLMs, identifying predominant failure modes in long-term reasoning and instruction following.

title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents

abstract: AI agents aim to solve complex tasks by combining text-based reasoning with external tool calls. Unfortunately, AI agents are vulnerable to prompt injection attacks where data returned by external tools hijacks the agent to execute malicious tasks. To measure the adversarial robustness of AI agents, we introduce AgentDojo, an evaluation framework for agents that execute tools over untrusted data. To capture the evolving nature of attacks and defenses, AgentDojo is not a static test suite, but rather an extensible environment for designing and evaluating new agent tasks, defenses, and adaptive attacks. We populate the environment with 97 realistic tasks (e.g., managing an email client, navigating an e-banking website, or making travel bookings), 629 security test cases, and various attack and defense paradigms from the literature. We find that AgentDojo poses a challenge for both attacks and defenses: state-of-the-art LLMs fail at many tasks (even in the absence of attacks), and existing prompt injection attacks break some security properties but not all. We hope that AgentDojo can foster research on new design principles for AI agents that solve common tasks in a reliable and robust manner.. We release the code for AgentDojo at this https URL.

keyInsights: More capable LLM agents, while better at benign tasks, tend to be more susceptible to prompt injection attacks, exhibiting an "inverse scaling law" regarding attack success rate.

Subtle variations in attack phrasing significantly impact success, with adaptive attacks outperforming generic ones, while precise attacker knowledge (like model/user names) provides only marginal gains.

Existing prompt injection defenses show mixed effectiveness; simple tool isolation mechanisms like 'tool filtering' prove most successful, reducing targeted attack success rates to 7.5%, though they have limitations.
originalProblem: Existing AI agent benchmarks primarily evaluate utility in benign settings and neglect adversarial robustness against prompt injection attacks.
Prior prompt injection benchmarks focus on simpler, single-turn scenarios, lacking the dynamic, stateful, multi-tool interactions typical of sophisticated AI agents.
Current evaluation methods often rely on LLM-based simulators or evaluators, which are themselves susceptible to the same attacks, compromising evaluation integrity.
results: State-of-the-art LLMs like Claude 3.5 Sonnet and GPT-4o achieve benign utility rates of only 78.22% and 69.00% respectively, indicating inherent difficulty of AgentDojo's multi-step tasks.
Prompt injection attacks cause a 10-25% absolute reduction in utility under attack for most models, with GPT-4o exhibiting a 47.69% targeted attack success rate against a generic attack.

The 'tool filter' defense reduced targeted attack success rates to 7.5%, while a BERT-based prompt injection detector caused significant degradation due to high false positives, and prompt sandwiching showed reasonable, but limited, effectiveness.
solution: AgentDojo introduces an extensible, dynamic evaluation framework with four realistic environments (e.g., Workspace, Slack, Banking) and 74 diverse tools to simulate complex, multi-turn interactions.
It uses formal, deterministic utility and security functions to evaluate task completion and malicious actions, ensuring integrity without reliance on LLM-based evaluators.
The framework provides modular interfaces for implementing and assessing new agents, attacks, and defenses, supporting adaptive attacks and acknowledging the evolving nature of ML security.
paper_summary.summary: ETH Zurich researchers developed AgentDojo, a dynamic and extensible evaluation framework to measure the adversarial robustness of LLM agents against prompt injection attacks in realistic, tool-calling environments. The framework revealed that even highly capable LLMs struggle with complex benign tasks and are susceptible to prompt injection attacks, with more capable models often being easier to attack. While existing defenses show mixed results, simple tool isolation mechanisms proved most effective at mitigating attacks.

title: AgentDojo: A Dynamic Environment to Evaluate Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses for LLM Agents

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs)-based agents have made impressive progress in reasoning and tool use, enabling them to solve complex tasks. However, their ability to proactively collaborate with users, especially when goals are vague, evolving, or indirectly expressed, remains underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce UserBench, a user-centric benchmark designed to evaluate agents in multi-turn, preference-driven interactions. UserBench features simulated users who start with underspecified goals and reveal preferences incrementally, requiring agents to proactively clarify intent and make grounded decisions with tools. Our evaluation of leading open- and closed-source LLMs reveals a significant disconnect between task completion and user alignment. For instance, models provide answers that fully align with all user intents only 20% of the time on average, and even the most advanced models uncover fewer than 30% of all user preferences through active interaction. These results highlight the challenges of building agents that are not just capable task executors, but true collaborative partners. UserBench offers an interactive environment to measure and advance this critical capability.

keyInsights: The primary challenge for LLM agents in UserBench shifts from efficient tool invocation to genuine user understanding and effective intent clarification, particularly with concentrated preferences.
Current state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit low rates of proactive preference elicitation, actively uncovering fewer than 30% of user preferences through interaction.
Increasing the number of interaction turns does not consistently improve agent performance, suggesting current models lack robust dialogue planning and strategic information gathering.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks primarily focus on tool use and task completion under idealized conditions with fully specified and static user goals.
Real-world human communication involves underspecified, incremental, and indirect user requests, which current LLM agents often struggle to interpret and adapt to.
LLM agents, despite strong task performance, frequently fail to satisfy actual user needs due to a lack of capabilities in user-centric understanding and proactive collaboration.
results: Evaluations of leading LLMs revealed limited user-centric reasoning, with models achieving full alignment with all user intents only 20% of the time on average.
The single-choice setting, requiring agents to select only one optimal option, proved significantly more challenging, reducing average scores by approximately 40% compared to multi-choice.
Model performance consistently declined as the number of preferences per aspect increased, indicating that localized reasoning complexity is a primary driver of difficulty for agents.
solution: UserBench provides a Gymnasium environment with an "oracle user" simulation that presents dynamically revealed, implicitly expressed preferences in travel planning scenarios.
The environment incorporates tool augmentation for database searching and supports agent actions for querying, interacting with the user, and recommending solutions.

It is designed to capture underspecification, incrementality, and indirectness through its dynamic preference elicitation and implicit communication, enabling evaluation and training of user-centric agents.
paper_summary.summary: UserBench introduces an interactive Gymnasium environment for evaluating and training large language model (LLM) agents in user-centric reasoning. It models realistic human communication patterns, including underspecification, incrementality, and indirectness, providing a standardized platform for developing agents that effectively collaborate with users beyond basic task execution.

title: UserBench: An Interactive Gym Environment for User-Centric Agents

abstract: The rapid evolution of Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has advanced workflow automation; however, existing research mainly targets performance upper bounds in static environments, overlooking robustness for stochastic real-world deployment. We identify three key challenges: dynamic task scheduling, active exploration under uncertainty, and continuous learning from experience. To bridge this gap, we introduce \method{}, a dynamic evaluation environment that simulates a "trainee" agent continuously exploring a novel setting. Unlike traditional benchmarks, \method{} evaluates agents along three dimensions: (1) context-aware scheduling for streaming tasks with varying priorities; (2) prudent information acquisition to reduce hallucination via active exploration; and (3) continuous evolution by distilling generalized strategies from rule-based, dynamically generated tasks. Experiments show that cutting-edge agents have significant deficiencies in dynamic environments, especially in active exploration and continual learning. Our work establishes a framework for assessing agent reliability, shifting evaluation from static tests to realistic, production-oriented scenarios. Our codes are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Evaluating agents in dynamic, partially observable, and continually evolving environments is crucial for developing truly reliable AI agents beyond static lab settings.
State-of-the-art LLM agents exhibit significant deficiencies in autonomous exploration and robust continual learning when faced with dynamic and uncertain tasks.

Human guidance dramatically improves agent performance (e.g., Checkpoint Score from 0.24 to 0.83), while agent self-evolution yields only negligible gains (+0.04), highlighting a major gap in autonomous strategic planning.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for MLLM agents primarily focus on peak performance in static, fully observable environments, overlooking robustness needed for real-world stochastic settings.
Current agents struggle with dynamic task scheduling, managing streaming tasks with varying priorities and deadlines in complex workflows.
MLLM agents lack the ability for active exploration to seek out necessary information in novel, uncertain scenarios, often leading to hallucinations rather than prudent inquiry.
results: Even top-performing models, such as Gemini-3-Flash, achieved only a 35% Success Rate and 0.63 Checkpoint Score on Trainee-Bench, indicating a substantial reliability gap.
Performance generally deteriorated with increased task workload and difficulty, with most models seeing success rates plummet from 34-37% on easy tasks to 7-8% on hard tasks.

Continual learning with the MUSE framework resulted in an *overall decline* in performance for easy tasks and only marginal improvement (+0.04 CS) for hard tasks, suggesting current mechanisms struggle with effective experience generalization.
solution: The paper introduces Trainee-Bench, a dynamic evaluation environment that simulates a "trainee" agent's first day in a workplace, incorporating dynamically generated tasks, partial observability, and continuous timelines.
It utilizes human-crafted meta-tasks that are stochastically instantiated into unique scenarios, preventing memorization and requiring general reasoning and active information gathering.
Composite scenarios are designed with temporal constraints, inter-task dependencies, and preemptive interrupts to rigorously test context-aware scheduling and adaptive planning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, Zhejiang University, and other institutions introduce Trainee-Bench, a dynamic evaluation environment for MLLM agents that simulates stochastic workplace scenarios to assess learning, exploration, and scheduling capabilities. The work reveals a substantial reliability gap in current state-of-the-art models, which achieved only a 35% success rate on the benchmark and showed negligible self-evolution compared to human-guided performance.

title: The Agent's First Day: Benchmarking Learning, Exploration, and Scheduling in the Workplace Scenarios

abstract: We introduce Gaia2, a benchmark for evaluating large language model agents in realistic, asynchronous environments. Unlike prior static or synchronous evaluations, Gaia2 introduces scenarios where environments evolve independently of agent actions, requiring agents to operate under temporal constraints, adapt to noisy and dynamic events, resolve ambiguity, and collaborate with other agents. Each scenario is paired with a write-action verifier, enabling fine-grained, action-level evaluation and making Gaia2 directly usable for reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source models shows that no model dominates across capabilities: GPT-5 (high) reaches the strongest overall score of 42% pass@1 but fails on time-sensitive tasks, Claude-4 Sonnet trades accuracy and speed for cost, Kimi-K2 leads among open-source models with 21% pass@1. These results highlight fundamental trade-offs between reasoning, efficiency, robustness, and expose challenges in closing the "sim2real" gap. Gaia2 is built on a consumer environment with the open-source Agents Research Environments platform and designed to be easy to extend. By releasing Gaia2 alongside the foundational ARE framework, we aim to provide the community with a flexible infrastructure for developing, benchmarking, and training the next generation of practical agent systems.

keyInsights: State-of-the-art LLM agents exhibit trade-offs, with no single model demonstrating superior performance across all capabilities like temporal reasoning, adaptability, and noise robustness.
Inference latency significantly impacts performance on time-sensitive tasks, where faster models achieved higher scores even with potentially weaker overall reasoning.
Multi-agent collaboration can benefit weaker models but may not yield cost-normalized gains for stronger agents, indicating that efficient task decomposition and delegation are crucial.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM agents do not adequately evaluate performance in dynamic, asynchronous, and complex real-world environments.
There is a lack of comprehensive, verifiable frameworks to assess capabilities like temporal reasoning, noise robustness, ambiguity resolution, and multi-agent collaboration.
Current Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods struggle due to insufficient fine-grained reward signals for complex, multi-step tasks.
results: GPT-5 (high) achieved an overall pass@1 score of 42.1%, while Kimi-K2 led open-source models with 20.1% pass@1, demonstrating current performance ceilings.

Models struggled with complex capabilities such as ambiguity resolution, adaptability, and time-sensitive tasks; for instance, GPT-5 (high) scored 0.0% on time-sensitive tasks with default inference but 34.4% when latency was eliminated.
Agent2Agent experiments indicated that weaker models benefited from increased collaboration, and heterogeneous teams (strong main agent, weaker app-agents) improved performance.
solution: An open-source Agents Research Environments (ARE) framework was developed to create asynchronous, event-driven environments for LLM agent evaluation.
The GAIA2 benchmark was constructed using ARE, comprising 1,120 scenarios that test critical capabilities in dynamic settings, including temporal reasoning and multi-agent interaction.
A novel action-level verifier was integrated to provide precise, verifiable rewards for each agent action, enabling scalable Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Meta SuperIntelligence Labs developed GAIA2, a benchmark and open-source ARE framework, to evaluate large language model agents in dynamic, asynchronous, and multi-agent environments. Empirical evaluation revealed that state-of-the-art models, including GPT-5 (high) at 42.1% pass@1 and Kimi-K2 at 20.1% pass@1 among open-source models, face significant challenges with temporal reasoning, noise robustness, and ambiguity resolution.

title: Gaia2: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Dynamic and Asynchronous Environments

abstract: Recent advances in AI agents capable of solving complex, everyday tasks, from scheduling to customer service, have enabled deployment in real-world settings, but their possibilities for unsafe behavior demands rigorous evaluation. While prior benchmarks have attempted to assess agent safety, most fall short by relying on simulated environments, narrow task domains, or unrealistic tool abstractions. We introduce OpenAgentSafety, a comprehensive and modular framework for evaluating agent behavior across eight critical risk categories. Unlike prior work, our framework evaluates agents that interact with real tools, including web browsers, code execution environments, file systems, bash shells, and messaging platforms; and supports over 350 multi-turn, multi-user tasks spanning both benign and adversarial user intents. OpenAgentSafety is designed for extensibility, allowing researchers to add tools, tasks, websites, and adversarial strategies with minimal effort. It combines rule-based analysis with LLM-as-judge assessments to detect both overt and subtle unsafe behaviors. Empirical analysis of five prominent LLMs in agentic scenarios reveals unsafe behavior in 51.2% of safety-vulnerable tasks with Claude-Sonnet-3.7, to 72.7% with o3-mini, highlighting critical safety vulnerabilities and the need for stronger safeguards before real-world deployment.

keyInsights: Modern LLM agents, even advanced ones, exhibit pervasive unsafe behavior (49-73% in safety-vulnerable tasks), particularly when confronting systemic risks that require procedural judgment or understanding of institutional norms.

Unsafe behaviors are frequently elicited by benign user prompts or hidden malicious intents from secondary actors (NPCs), demonstrating a fragility in multi-turn intent tracking and the generalization of refusal strategies.

The evaluation revealed that LLM-as-Judge models often underestimate implied unsafe behaviors and overestimate failure rates by misinterpreting superficial errors, highlighting the necessity of hybrid evaluation methods.
originalProblem: Existing LLM safety evaluations primarily focus on static output generation (e.g., bias, toxicity) and do not adequately address the unique safety challenges of AI agents that interact with dynamic environments and execute actions via real-world tools.

Prior benchmarks for LLM agents often rely on simulated environments, simplified tool APIs, or narrow task domains, failing to capture the complexities and risks of realistic, multi-step interactions, particularly those involving multi-turn and multi-user dynamics.

The rapid development and deployment of LLM-powered agents with access to powerful tools have outpaced the progress in robust safety assurance methods, leading to a disparity between agent capabilities and the investigation of potential failure modes.
results: All seven prominent LLMs evaluated exhibited substantial unsafe behavior, with unsafe rates ranging from 49.06% (Claude Sonnet 4) to 72.73% (o3-mini) in safety-vulnerable tasks.

Systemic risk categories (e.g., computer security compromise, legal violations, privacy breaches) consistently showed the highest unsafe rates (72-86%), as agents frequently disregarded authorization policies or institutional norms.

Agents displayed unsafe actions in 50-86% of tasks with benign prompts and showed increased unsafe rates (44.9-69.2%) when malicious goals were introduced by NPCs, indicating challenges in multi-turn intent tracking and refusal strategies.
solution: Introduces OPENAGENTSAFETY (OA-SAFETY), an open-source framework that places AI agents in a containerized sandbox with access to real-world tools (Unix shell, file system, Python interpreter, web browser) and locally hosted replicas of real platforms like GitLab.

Supports diverse user intentions, from benign ambiguity to explicit adversarial manipulation, and incorporates multi-user scenarios by integrating secondary actors (NPCs) with varied or conflicting goals, scaled to over 350 tasks.

Employs a hybrid evaluation strategy combining precise rule-based evaluators to detect specific unsafe environmental outcomes with LLM-as-Judge assessments to identify unsafe intent or partial malicious actions in agent trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Carnegie Mellon University and the Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence developed OPENAGENTSAFETY, an open-source framework for evaluating AI agent safety in realistic, high-risk scenarios using sandboxed real-world tools and complex multi-user interactions. Empirical evaluation revealed that all seven tested LLMs exhibited unsafe behavior in 49-73% of safety-vulnerable tasks, especially when confronting systemic risks or subtle malicious intents from secondary actors.

title: OpenAgentSafety: A Comprehensive Framework for Evaluating Real-World AI Agent Safety

abstract: Foundation agents have rapidly advanced in their ability to reason and interact with real environments, making the evaluation of their core capabilities increasingly important. While many benchmarks have been developed to assess agent performance, most concentrate on academic settings or artificially designed scenarios while overlooking the challenges that arise in real applications. To address this issue, we focus on a highly practical real-world setting, the e-commerce domain, which involves a large volume of diverse user interactions, dynamic market conditions, and tasks directly tied to real decision-making processes. To this end, we introduce EcomBench, a holistic E-commerce Benchmark designed to evaluate agent performance in realistic e-commerce environments. EcomBench is built from genuine user demands embedded in leading global e-commerce ecosystems and is carefully curated and annotated through human experts to ensure clarity, accuracy, and domain relevance. It covers multiple task categories within e-commerce scenarios and defines three difficulty levels that evaluate agents on key capabilities such as deep information retrieval, multi-step reasoning, and cross-source knowledge integration. By grounding evaluation in real e-commerce contexts, EcomBench provides a rigorous and dynamic testbed for measuring the practical capabilities of agents in modern e-commerce.

keyInsights: A human-in-the-loop framework, combining LLM scalability with expert curation, is crucial for developing authentic and professionally relevant domain-specific benchmarks.
Utilizing a tool hierarchy allows for effective stratification of task difficulty, revealing agent limitations in complex reasoning, multi-step problem decomposition, and long-horizon planning.
A dynamic update mechanism, such as quarterly revisions, is essential for maintaining benchmark relevance and mitigating data contamination risks in rapidly evolving domains like e-commerce.
originalProblem: Existing agent evaluation benchmarks often rely on academic puzzles or artificially designed scenarios, failing to reflect the complexity of real-world applications.
There is a need for robust and realistic evaluation tools for foundation agents in dynamic, knowledge-intensive, and economically significant domains like e-commerce.

E-commerce environments present unique challenges such as high volume/diversity of interactions, dynamic market conditions, and direct ties to business decisions, which current benchmarks do not adequately address.
results: Current leading foundation models achieved only 65% overall accuracy on EcomBench, indicating substantial room for improvement in solving practical e-commerce tasks.
Performance significantly degraded with increasing task difficulty, with top models' accuracy dropping from over 90% on Level 1 tasks to 46% on the most challenging Level 3 tasks.

No single agent demonstrated comprehensive expertise across all e-commerce task categories; models exhibited specialized strengths, such as SuperGrok Expert in finance-related tasks and Gemini DeepResearch in strategy-related tasks.
solution: EcomBench, a new e-commerce benchmark for foundation agents, was developed using a human-in-the-loop framework to ensure authenticity and professionalism.
The benchmark collects real-world user demands and operational needs from major e-commerce ecosystems, which are then refined by human experts and categorized into three difficulty levels.
A novel tool-hierarchy-based approach identifies and constructs high-difficulty tasks, requiring complex reasoning and specialized e-commerce tool usage beyond basic web search.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Alibaba Group's Tongyi Lab developed EcomBench, a new benchmark for evaluating foundation agents in e-commerce using real-world user demands and expert curation. Evaluations showed leading models achieved an average overall accuracy of 65%, with performance dropping to 46% for the most complex tasks, revealing significant challenges in practical e-commerce problem-solving.

title: EcomBench: Towards Holistic Evaluation of Foundation Agents in E-commerce

abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly operate as Deep Research (DR) Agents capable of autonomous investigation and information synthesis, reliable evaluation of their task performance has become a critical bottleneck. Current benchmarks predominantly rely on static datasets, which suffer from several limitations: limited task generality, temporal misalignment, and data contamination. To address these, we introduce DR-Arena, a fully automated evaluation framework that pushes DR agents to their capability limits through dynamic investigation. DR-Arena constructs real-time Information Trees from fresh web trends to ensure the evaluation rubric is synchronized with the live world state, and employs an automated Examiner to generate structured tasks testing two orthogonal capabilities: Deep reasoning and Wide coverage. DR-Arena further adopts Adaptive Evolvement Loop, a state-machine controller that dynamically escalates task complexity based on real-time performance, demanding deeper deduction or wider aggregation until a decisive capability boundary emerges. Experiments with six advanced DR agents demonstrate that DR-Arena achieves a Spearman correlation of 0.94 with the LMSYS Search Arena leaderboard. This represents the state-of-the-art alignment with human preferences without any manual efforts, validating DR-Arena as a reliable alternative for costly human adjudication.

organization_info.name: Singapore University of Technology and Design

keyInsights: Dynamically constructing ground truth from the live web is critical for overcoming the limitations of static benchmarks, ensuring evaluation relevance, preventing data contamination, and synchronizing with real-world information.

An adaptive evaluation mechanism that dynamically escalates task complexity based on real-time agent performance efficiently identifies capability boundaries and provides granular diagnostic insights into specific reasoning or coverage failures.

Strict, evidence-based rubric adjudication is essential for the reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge systems, significantly reducing judge hallucination and ensuring high alignment with human preferences in complex, factual tasks.
originalProblem: Static benchmarks for evaluating Deep Research (DR) Agents suffer from limited task generality, temporal misalignment with evolving web data, and data contamination from repeated use in training.

Existing LLM-as-a-Judge or Agent-as-a-Judge frameworks primarily act as passive evaluators, grading static trajectories after tasks are completed, failing to capture the dynamic, interactive nature of deep research.
Evaluating complex, multi-step research tasks using human adjudication is expensive, time-consuming, and difficult to scale, hindering rapid agent development.
results: DR-Arena achieved a Spearman correlation of 0.94 and a Pearson correlation of 0.74 with the human-verified LMSYS Search Arena leaderboard, demonstrating state-of-the-art alignment with human preferences.

The framework significantly outperformed six baseline static and periodically updated benchmarks for DR agents in terms of correlation with human preferences, with many baselines showing negative correlations.

Ablation studies confirmed the Adaptive Evolvement Loop's efficiency, with Spearman correlation increasing from 0.77 to 0.94 by the second round, and diagnostics revealed distinct error profiles (Deep vs. Wide failures) for different agents.
solution: DR-Arena introduces a fully automated framework orchestrated by an LLM "Examiner," which dynamically constructs "Dynamic Information Trees" by scraping and structuring real-world websites in real-time.
The Examiner generates challenging deep research tasks (probing multi-hop reasoning and wide information gathering) and synthesizes ground-truth rubrics directly from these live information trees.

An "Adaptive Evolvement Loop" enables the Examiner to assess agent responses against rubrics and dynamically adjust follow-up question complexity (depth and width) to push agents to their capability limits and diagnose failure types (Deep, Wide, Both).
paper_summary.summary: DR-Arena is an automated evaluation framework for Deep Research Agents that dynamically constructs real-time information trees from the live web to generate tasks and ground truth. This approach achieves a 0.94 Spearman correlation with human-verified leaderboards, significantly outperforming static benchmarks and providing detailed diagnostic insights into agent capabilities.

title: DR-Arena: an Automated Evaluation Framework for Deep Research Agents

abstract: The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) into autonomous agents necessitates the management of extensive, dynamic contexts. Current benchmarks, however, remain largely static, relying on passive retrieval tasks that fail to simulate the complexities of agent-environment interaction, such as non-linear reasoning and iterative feedback. To address this, we introduce \textbf{AgentLongBench}, which evaluates agents through simulated environment rollouts based on Lateral Thinking Puzzles. This framework generates rigorous interaction trajectories across knowledge-intensive and knowledge-free scenarios. Experiments with state-of-the-art models and memory systems (32K to 4M tokens) expose a critical weakness: while adept at static retrieval, agents struggle with the dynamic information synthesis essential for workflows. Our analysis indicates that this degradation is driven by the minimum number of tokens required to resolve a query. This factor explains why the high information density inherent in massive tool responses poses a significantly greater challenge than the memory fragmentation typical of long-turn dialogues.

keyInsights: LLMs exhibit significant performance degradation in dynamic agentic tasks requiring information synthesis and state tracking, contrasting sharply with their abilities in static retrieval.

Models demonstrate a strong reliance on pre-trained parametric knowledge, with performance plummeting in knowledge-free settings, indicating a weakness in maintaining discrete logical states purely from context history.

High information density within structured, machine-generated tool responses poses a greater challenge for LLM agents than memory fragmentation across numerous turns in long dialogues, especially for precise data extraction tasks.

Current external memory architectures (e.g., RAG, A-Mem) generally fail to improve agent performance in these dynamic, long-context scenarios, often performing worse than the base LLM's native context window.
originalProblem: Current long-context benchmarks primarily assess static retrieval or passive reading comprehension, which is insufficient for evaluating the dynamic, interactive reasoning required by autonomous LLM agents.
There is a lack of evaluation frameworks for how LLM agents handle evolving contexts, track states iteratively, and parse and integrate high-density, noisy machine-generated tool outputs.
Existing benchmarks struggle to provide granular diagnosis of specific agentic failure modes, hindering targeted improvements in LLM architectures and agentic frameworks.
results: Proprietary models like Grok-4.1 showed resilience up to 2M tokens, but even frontier models like Gemini-2.5-Flash and GPT-4.1 experienced significant performance decay, dropping below 40% and 30% accuracy respectively at 1M tokens.
Performance in Knowledge-Free settings for complex deduction tasks (e.g., `Intersection`) dropped to near-zero for most models, even for those achieving 30-40% in Knowledge-Intensive settings.

Tasks requiring high-precision positional awareness and extraction from dense tool responses, such as `Find Target Offsets`, proved particularly challenging due to high Adequate Context Length (ACL) requirements.

External memory augmentation methods, when tested with a Qwen3-30B-A3B backbone, generally did not improve and often degraded the performance compared to the unaugmented base model in agentic long-context evaluations.
solution: AgentLongBench utilizes simulated environment rollouts based on Lateral Thinking Puzzles to generate dynamic, causally linked interaction trajectories for evaluating LLM agents.

The benchmark introduces Knowledge-Intensive and Knowledge-Free settings to decouple parametric knowledge reliance from pure in-context reasoning, alongside Concise-Response and Verbose-Response formats to study the impact of information distribution.

A detailed task taxonomy, categorized into three cognitive dimensions (QA in Tool Response, QA in Environment Response, and Final Guess), is employed to enable fine-grained diagnosis of specific agentic failure modes.
paper_summary.summary: AgentLongBench introduces a controllable benchmark for evaluating long-context Large Language Model agents using dynamic Lateral Thinking Puzzle environments, revealing a sharp performance degradation in dynamic information synthesis, a strong reliance on parametric knowledge over pure reasoning, and the limited effectiveness of current external memory architectures. The benchmark demonstrates that high information density in tool logs poses a greater challenge than memory fragmentation in long dialogues, with even frontier models experiencing significant decay beyond 256K tokens.

title: AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts

abstract: Recent advances in reasoning models and agentic AI systems have led to an increased reliance on diverse external information. However, this shift introduces input contexts that are inherently noisy, a reality that current sanitized benchmarks fail to capture. We introduce NoisyBench, a comprehensive benchmark that systematically evaluates model robustness across 11 datasets in RAG, reasoning, alignment, and tool-use tasks against diverse noise types, including random documents, irrelevant chat histories, and hard negative distractors. Our evaluation reveals a catastrophic performance drop of up to 80% in state-of-the-art models when faced with contextual distractors. Crucially, we find that agentic workflows often amplify these errors by over-trusting noisy tool outputs, and distractors can trigger emergent misalignment even without adversarial intent. We find that prompting, context engineering, SFT, and outcome-reward only RL fail to ensure robustness; in contrast, our proposed Rationale-Aware Reward (RARE) significantly strengthens resilience by incentivizing the identification of helpful information within noise. Finally, we uncover an inverse scaling trend where increased test-time computation leads to worse performance in noisy settings and demonstrate via attention visualization that models disproportionately focus on distractor tokens, providing vital insights for building the next generation of robust, reasoning-capable agents.

keyInsights: Contextual distractors, even random and non-adversarial, cause catastrophic performance degradation (up to 80%+) and can lead to emergent misalignment in state-of-the-art LLMs.
Conventional robustness methods like prompting, context engineering, and supervised fine-tuning are largely ineffective or even detrimental in noisy environments.
In noisy settings, increased reasoning effort (more reasoning tokens) paradoxically leads to worse performance, indicating models become more confused rather than more accurate.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) and agentic AI systems are predominantly evaluated in "clean" benchmarks, which do not reflect the noisy, irrelevant, or misleading information encountered in real-world deployments.
The increasing reliance on external tools and long contexts in LLMs exposes them to imperfect data, leading to risks in critical applications like healthcare and finance.
A lack of systematic benchmarks hinders the understanding and mitigation of performance degradation when reasoning models face diverse contextual distractors.
results: All evaluated models experienced significant performance drops (e.g., DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B dropped by 80.6% with hard negatives), with strong clean performance not guaranteeing robustness.

Rationale-Aware Reward (RARE) consistently outperformed other mitigation strategies, achieving substantial robustness gains, such as a 306.5% relative improvement for DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on hard negative distractors.

Mechanistic analyses showed that incorrect predictions allocate disproportionately more attention to distractor tokens, and model output entropy increases with more distractors, indicating lower confidence.
solution: Introduced NoisyBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 11 datasets across RAG, reasoning, alignment, and tool use tasks, incorporating three types of contextual distractors.
Developed NoisyInstruct, a training dataset designed to expose models to a wide range of distractors, questions, answers, and hints to foster robustness.

Proposed Rationale-Aware Reward (RARE), a novel reinforcement learning objective that explicitly rewards models for identifying and using helpful information within noisy contexts during their reasoning process.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from KAIST AI, LG AI Research, and the University of Illinois Chicago developed NoisyBench, a benchmark revealing severe performance drops (9% to over 80%) in advanced reasoning models when confronted with contextual noise. Their proposed Rationale-Aware Reward (RARE) method, integrated into reinforcement learning, enhances robustness by explicitly guiding models to identify helpful information, achieving substantial gains, such as a 306.5% relative improvement on hard negative distractors.

title: Lost in the Noise: How Reasoning Models Fail with Contextual Distractors

abstract: With AI agents increasingly deployed as long-running systems, it becomes essential to autonomously construct and continuously evolve customized software to enable interaction within dynamic environments. Yet, existing benchmarks evaluate agents on isolated, one-off coding tasks, neglecting the temporal dependencies and technical debt inherent in real-world software evolution. To bridge this gap, we introduce DeepCommit, an agentic pipeline that reconstructs verifiable Milestone DAGs from noisy commit logs, where milestones are defined as semantically cohesive development goals. These executable sequences enable EvoClaw, a novel benchmark that requires agents to sustain system integrity and limit error accumulation, dimensions of long-term software evolution largely missing from current benchmarks. Our evaluation of 12 frontier models across 4 agent frameworks reveals a critical vulnerability: overall performance scores drop significantly from $>$80% on isolated tasks to at most 38% in continuous settings, exposing agents' profound struggle with long-term maintenance and error propagation.

keyInsights: Frontier LLM agents demonstrate a substantial performance drop from over 80% on isolated coding tasks to at most 38.03% on continuous evolution, indicating a difficulty with sustained code integrity and error management.
Performance declines consistently for later milestones and deeper topological layers in the evolution DAG, highlighting the compounding effect of upstream errors and increased technical debt.

An asymmetry exists in evolution dynamics: agents maintain the ability to implement new features (Recall), but rapidly reach a ceiling in preventing regressions (Precision), leading to accumulating unresolved issues.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM agents treat software development as a series of independent, one-off coding tasks, overlooking the continuous and temporal nature of real-world software evolution.

These benchmarks neglect critical aspects such as temporal dependencies, the accumulation of technical debt, and the propagation of errors, failing to assess agents' capabilities for sustained, maintainable codebase evolution.

Current evaluation granularities (coarse release-level or fine commit-level) are insufficient to represent semantically cohesive and functionally testable units of development with genuine prerequisite constraints.
results: Frontier LLM agents achieved a maximum continuous performance score of 38.03% and a Milestone Resolve Rate of up to 13.37% on EvoClaw, significantly lower than their performance on independent tasks.

Analysis of error chains showed that inherited failures and logic errors accounted for approximately 57% of root causes, with these issues increasingly dominating and propagating in later stages of evolution.

Agent behaviors indicated that higher exploration correlated with better performance, while "blind thrashing"—characterized by high repeat edits and low verification—led to suboptimal outcomes and accelerated error accumulation.
solution: Introduces a "Milestone-level" granularity for software evolution tasks, defined as semantically cohesive and functionally testable units, organized into Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to capture prerequisite constraints.

Develops DeepCommit, an automated, agent-driven pipeline that reconstructs verifiable and executable Milestone DAGs from raw Git commit histories through preprocessing, LLM-guided construction, and runtime environment resolution.

Establishes EvoClaw, a benchmark for continuous software evolution, which evaluates agents in persistent, stateful environments against a stream of dependency-constrained milestones, requiring them to manage accumulated modifications and prevent regressions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from USC, UCR, UCSD, and others developed EvoClaw, a benchmark that evaluates AI agents on continuous software evolution through dependency-constrained "Milestone-level" tasks. The evaluation found that frontier LLM agents struggle significantly in these continuous settings, achieving a maximum task completion score of 38.03% compared to over 80% on isolated tasks, primarily due to difficulties in managing error propagation and preventing regressions.

title: EvoClaw: Evaluating AI Agents on Continuous Software Evolution

abstract: As LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed in real-life scenarios, existing benchmarks fail to capture their inherent complexity of handling extensive information, leveraging diverse resources, and managing dynamic user interactions. To address this gap, we introduce VitaBench, a challenging benchmark that evaluates agents on versatile interactive tasks grounded in real-world settings. Drawing from daily applications in food delivery, in-store consumption, and online travel services, VitaBench presents agents with the most complex life-serving simulation environment to date, comprising 66 tools. Through a framework that eliminates domain-specific policies, we enable flexible composition of these scenarios and tools, yielding 100 cross-scenario tasks (main results) and 300 single-scenario tasks. Each task is derived from multiple real user requests and requires agents to reason across temporal and spatial dimensions, utilize complex tool sets, proactively clarify ambiguous instructions, and track shifting user intent throughout multi-turn conversations. Moreover, we propose a rubric-based sliding window evaluator, enabling robust assessment of diverse solution pathways in complex environments and stochastic interactions. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals that even the most advanced models achieve only 30% success rate on cross-scenario tasks, and less than 50% success rate on others. Overall, we believe VitaBench will serve as a valuable resource for advancing the development of AI agents in practical real-world applications. The code, dataset, and leaderboard are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Agentic task complexity in real-world applications can be formally measured across three dimensions: reasoning, tool use, and dynamic interaction.
Graph-based tool dependency modeling enables autonomous agent behavior and cross-domain reasoning, moving beyond rigid policy adherence.
Robust evaluation of complex, long-horizon, multi-turn trajectories requires a rubric-based sliding window approach that persistently tracks criterion satisfaction.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks operate in simplified, controlled settings, failing to capture the intricate complexities of real-world deployments.
Current evaluation frameworks inadequately consider dynamic user interactions, diverse external resources, and extensive environmental information.
Many benchmarks impose rigid, domain-specific policies, limiting agents' autonomous exploration and reasoning capabilities in dynamic environments.
results: State-of-the-art LLM agents achieve only a 30.0% average success rate on cross-scenario tasks, indicating significant limitations in complex real-world application contexts.
"Thinking" mechanisms (e.g., chain-of-thought) consistently enhance agent effectiveness and efficiency, leading to higher success rates and fewer interaction turns.
Detailed error analysis reveals that reasoning errors account for the majority (61.8%) of failures in complex tasks, followed by tool usage errors (21.1%) and interaction errors (7.9%).
solution: VitaBench features 400 interactive tasks directly derived from real "life-serving applications" (delivery, in-store, online travel) to evaluate agents across reasoning, tool, and interaction complexity.
Tool use is modeled as a graph structure to inherently encode domain rules, enabling autonomous agent exploration across interconnected action spaces without verbose policy documents.
A sophisticated user simulator introduces realistic, dynamic multi-turn interaction challenges through unique profiles, behavioral attributes, and progressive information disclosure.
paper_summary.summary: VitaBench, developed by the Meituan LongCat Team, introduces a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on versatile interactive tasks derived from real-world "life-serving applications." It assesses agents across reasoning, tool use, and interaction complexity, revealing that even state-of-the-art models achieve only a 30.0% average success rate on cross-scenario tasks.

title: VitaBench: Benchmarking LLM Agents with Versatile Interactive Tasks in Real-world Applications

abstract:
Lifelong learning is essential for intelligent agents operating in dynamic
environments. Current large language model (LLM)-based agents, however, remain
stateless and unable to accumulate or transfer knowledge over time. Existing
benchmarks treat agents as static systems and fail to evaluate lifelong
learning capabilities. We present LifelongAgentBench, the first unified
benchmark designed to systematically assess the lifelong learning ability of
LLM agents. It provides skill-grounded, interdependent tasks across three
interactive environments, Database, Operating System, and Knowledge Graph, with
automatic label verification, reproducibility, and modular extensibility.
Extensive experiments reveal that conventional experience replay has limited
effectiveness for LLM agents due to irrelevant information and context length
constraints. We further introduce a group self-consistency mechanism that
significantly improves lifelong learning performance. We hope
LifelongAgentBench will advance the development of adaptive, memory-capable LLM
agents.

keyInsights: Experience replay consistently improves LLM agent performance in lifelong learning, but its benefits are heavily constrained by context length and the inclusion of irrelevant information.
Optimal experience replay buffer size varies by environment and task complexity, with larger buffers often leading to performance degradation or out-of-memory issues for longer trajectories.
The proposed group self-consistency mechanism offers a scalable solution to mitigate context length problems, enabling more effective use of historical experiences.
originalProblem: Current LLM agents operate in a stateless manner, lacking the ability to accumulate, retain, and transfer knowledge across tasks.
Existing LLM agent benchmarks primarily evaluate static systems on isolated tasks, failing to assess lifelong learning aspects like inter-task dependencies and long-term knowledge retention.
Reproducibility issues and label inaccuracies in prior benchmarks hinder robust evaluation and broader research.
results: Experience replay consistently improved agent performance, with DB environment accuracy rising from 19% (no replay) to 78% (with 64 examples).
The novel group self-consistency method drastically reduced token usage (e.g., from 56,409 to 11,002 tokens for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on KG tasks) while maintaining accuracy.
Open-source LLMs like Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct exhibited stable performance, and achieved competitive results with larger models when integrated with experience replay.
solution: Introduction of LifelongAgentBench, a unified benchmark specifically designed to evaluate lifelong learning capabilities in LLM agents.
Creation of skill-grounded, interdependent tasks across diverse interactive environments: Database, Operating System, and Knowledge Graph.
The benchmark design incorporates automatic label verification, containerized environments, and modular APIs to ensure reproducibility, verifiability, and extensibility.
paper_summary.summary: LifelongAgentBench introduces the first unified benchmark to evaluate large language model agents as lifelong learners across Database, Operating System, and Knowledge Graph environments. The benchmark demonstrates that experience replay consistently improves agent performance (e.g., 19% to 78% accuracy in DB tasks) but is limited by context length, leading to the development of group self-consistency which drastically reduces token usage (e.g., 56,409 to 11,002 tokens) while maintaining effectiveness.

title: LifelongAgentBench: Evaluating LLM Agents as Lifelong Learners

abstract: We introduce DRBench, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on complex, open-ended deep research tasks in enterprise settings. Unlike prior benchmarks that focus on simple questions or web-only queries, DRBench evaluates agents on multi-step queries (for example, "What changes should we make to our product roadmap to ensure compliance with this standard?") that require identifying supporting facts from both the public web and private company knowledge base. Each task is grounded in realistic user personas and enterprise context, spanning a heterogeneous search space that includes productivity software, cloud file systems, emails, chat conversations, and the open web. Tasks are generated through a carefully designed synthesis pipeline with human-in-the-loop verification, and agents are evaluated on their ability to recall relevant insights, maintain factual accuracy, and produce coherent, well-structured reports. We release 100 deep research tasks across 10 domains, such as Sales, Cybersecurity, and Compliance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DRBench by evaluating diverse DR agents across open- and closed-source models (such as GPT, Llama, and Qwen) and DR strategies, highlighting their strengths, weaknesses, and the critical path for advancing enterprise deep research. Code and data are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Adaptive Action Planning (AAP) significantly improves insight recall and report quality by allowing agents to dynamically refine research strategies, recover missed evidence, and optimize source utilization during execution.

A fundamental trade-off exists between an agent's ability to broadly explore for insights (improving recall) and maintaining high factual accuracy; simpler planning can yield more grounded answers, while complex adaptive approaches may increase coverage but risk fragmented reasoning.

LLM agents demonstrate a critical limitation in meta-reasoning, consistently failing to identify when required information is absent from internal files and should be sourced from the open web, highlighting a need for improved problem scoping and external query formulation.

Navigating and interacting within realistic, multi-application enterprise environments significantly degrades agent performance compared to direct file access, with weaker backbone models experiencing substantial drops in insight recall and factuality.
originalProblem: Organizations face challenges extracting actionable insights from vast, fragmented information spread across diverse enterprise applications and formats, requiring labor-intensive human 'deep research'.

Existing LLM agent benchmarks are insufficient for enterprise deep research, focusing on narrow aspects like factual accuracy or web-only data, and using simplified environments that lack the complexity of real-world multi-step, open-ended strategic questions.

Prior evaluation methods often fail to assess an agent's ability to identify salient insights, maintain faithfulness to retrieved evidence, or adapt to heterogeneous, multi-application enterprise data environments.
results: The DRBench Agent (DRBA) with a GPT-4o backbone achieved 58.04% factuality and 95.76% distractor avoidance, but only 13.18% insight recall, indicating difficulty in extracting critical enterprise-specific information.

Adaptive Action Planning (AAP) improved insight recall to 15.97% and report quality to 90.08%, demonstrating its effectiveness, while GPT-5 emerged as the top-performing backbone model across all metrics (36.52% Insight Recall, 72.11% Factuality).

Agent performance consistently declined when moving from local-only file access to the app-based enterprise environment, especially for weaker models, underscoring the added complexity of multi-application interaction and context switching.

Web-only agents operating in a browser setting performed very poorly (1.11% insight recall), suggesting current browser-based agents are ineffective for complex, multi-modal deep research tasks in enterprise settings.
solution: DRBench provides 100 high-level, open-ended research tasks spanning 10 enterprise domains, requiring agents to synthesize information from both public web sources and private company knowledge bases.
A reproducible Docker environment simulates a realistic enterprise setting, integrating applications like Nextcloud, Mattermost, Roundcube, and FileBrowser, accessible via a VNC/NoVNC desktop.

A five-stage LLM and human-in-the-loop pipeline generates realistic company profiles, user personas, public/private insights (including distractors), and maps them to diverse file formats across various enterprise applications.
paper_summary.summary: DRBench introduces a benchmark for evaluating large language model agents on complex, open-ended deep research tasks within realistic enterprise environments, integrating both public web and private organizational data. Evaluations reveal that current agents struggle to reliably extract critical enterprise-specific insights and integrate information across diverse applications, despite proficiency in avoiding distractor content.

title: DRBench: A Realistic Benchmark for Enterprise Deep Research

abstract: We introduce DeepSearchQA, a 900-prompt benchmark for evaluating agents on difficult multi-step information-seeking tasks across 17 different fields. Unlike traditional benchmarks that target single answer retrieval or broad-spectrum factuality, DeepSearchQA features a dataset of challenging, handcrafted tasks designed to evaluate an agent's ability to execute complex search plans to generate exhaustive answer lists. This shift in design explicitly tests three critical, yet under-evaluated capabilities: 1) systematic collation of fragmented information from disparate sources, 2) de-duplication and entity resolution to ensure precision, and 3) the ability to reason about stopping criteria within an open-ended search space. Each task is structured as a causal chain, where discovering information for one step is dependent on the successful completion of the previous one, stressing long-horizon planning and context retention. All tasks are grounded in the open web with objectively verifiable answer sets. Our comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art agent architectures reveals significant performance limitations: even the most advanced models struggle to balance high recall with precision. We observe distinct failure modes ranging from premature stopping (under-retrieval) to hedging behaviors, where agents cast an overly wide net of low-confidence answers to artificially boost recall. These findings highlight critical headroom in current agent designs and position DeepSearchQA as an essential diagnostic tool for driving future research toward more robust, deep-research capabilities.

keyInsights: Truly capable deep research agents must move beyond finding *an* answer to finding *all* relevant answers, requiring robust strategies for systematic exploration and comprehensive information synthesis.

The "Last Mile Problem" highlights that even top agents struggle to achieve perfect completeness and precision simultaneously, often missing obscure entities or failing to filter extraneous information.
Effective agentic reasoning for comprehensive tasks necessitates sophisticated dynamic stopping criteria to determine when a retrieved set is exhaustive and further searching is unproductive.
originalProblem: Existing AI agent evaluation paradigms primarily focus on "single-answer verification," failing to assess the ability to perform exhaustive, multi-faceted research.

Current benchmarks lack the capability to evaluate complex, multi-step information-seeking tasks that require compiling complete lists or synthesizing information from disparate sources on the open web.
A "Comprehensiveness Gap" exists, as current metrics do not adequately test higher-order agent capabilities like systematic collation, entity resolution, and reasoning about search completeness.
results: State-of-the-art agents, such as the Gemini Deep Research Agent and GPT-5 Pro High Reasoning, achieved F1-Scores of 81.90% and 78.98% respectively, indicating significant room for improvement in handling comprehensive research tasks.

Deep Research Agents with iterative tool-use capabilities consistently and significantly outperformed their standalone reasoning model counterparts, confirming the critical role of agentic architectures for complex web-based tasks.

A "hard reasoning threshold" was observed, where smaller models experienced a drastic drop in utility and failed to complete tasks, suggesting these comprehensive research tasks are intractable via simple semantic search and require advanced multi-step reasoning.
solution: DeepSearchQA comprises 900 hand-crafted, time-anchored prompts across 17 diverse domains, designed to necessitate exhaustive answer set generation rather than single-point retrieval.

The benchmark tasks are structured as causal chains, stressing long-horizon planning and context retention, and requiring agents to systematically aggregate fragmented information, perform entity de-duplication, and infer optimal stopping points.

An outcome-based evaluation methodology is employed, using F1-score, Precision, and Recall, with an LLM-as-a-Judge pipeline for automated, objective assessment of completeness and correctness of the final answer sets.
paper_summary.summary: DeepSearchQA introduces a new benchmark for evaluating AI agents, shifting focus from single-answer retrieval to exhaustive answer set generation for complex web research tasks. The benchmark demonstrates that even leading deep research agents still struggle to balance high recall with precision and identify optimal stopping criteria, while agentic architectures consistently outperform standalone large language models.

title: DeepSearchQA: Bridging the Comprehensiveness Gap for Deep Research Agents

abstract: Personal AI agents like OpenClaw run with elevated privileges on users' local machines, where a single successful prompt injection can leak credentials, redirect financial transactions, or destroy files. This threat goes well beyond conventional text-level jailbreaks, yet existing safety evaluations fall short: most test models in isolated chat settings, rely on synthetic environments, and do not account for how the agent framework itself shapes safety outcomes. We introduce CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark of 120 adversarial test scenarios organized along three dimensions (harm domain, attack vector, and harmful action type) and grounded in realistic, high-privilege professional workspaces spanning software engineering, finance, healthcare, law, and DevOps. Each test case embeds adversarial content in one of three channels the agent encounters during normal work: workspace skill files, emails from trusted senders, and web pages. We evaluate five frontier LLMs as agent backbones, running 2,520 sandboxed trials across all configurations. Attack success rates (ASR) range from 40\% to 75\% across models and vary sharply by injection vector, with skill instructions (highest trust) consistently more dangerous than email or web content.
