Action-trace analysis reveals that the strongest model maintains hard boundaries against credential forwarding and destructive actions, while weaker models permit both. Cross-scaffold experiments on three agent frameworks further demonstrate that safety is not determined by the backbone model alone but depends on the full deployment stack, calling for safety evaluation that treats model and framework as joint variables. Code and data will be available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Chat-level safety of LLMs does not reliably translate to action-level safety in autonomous agents; models safe in chat can still perform harmful actions via tool calls.
Agent safety is a system-level property influenced not only by the backbone LLM but also significantly by the agent framework's scaffolding, tool orchestration, and memory management.

Attack vectors exhibit a trust-level gradient (skill > email > web injection), with agent defenses often being intent-sensitive (e.g., differentiating imperative vs. declarative phrasing) rather than purely content-sensitive.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents powered by LLMs operate with elevated privileges on user machines, creating a larger attack surface susceptible to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks that can cause real-world harm.

Prior safety research primarily focused on text-level vulnerabilities and isolated chat environments, failing to quantify the compliance gap between chat-level safety and agent-level action execution.
Existing benchmarks often rely on synthetic environments and do not jointly consider the LLM backbone and agent framework as co-variables, obscuring their combined influence on adversarial risk.
results: Frontier LLMs exhibited overall Attack Success Rates (ASR) ranging from 40% to 75% against indirect prompt injections, with Claude Sonnet 4.6 demonstrating the lowest ASR (40.0%) and GPT-5.1 the highest (75.0%).
A consistent trust-level gradient was observed across injection vectors on OpenClaw, with skill injection achieving the highest ASR (69.4%) and web injection the lowest (38.4%).

Agent scaffold choice alone shifted Claude Sonnet 4.6's overall ASR by 8.6 percentage points (from 40.0% to 48.6%), and reversed the trust-level gradient for email vs. skill injection on different frameworks.
solution: Introduced CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark designed to evaluate personal AI agents against realistic indirect prompt injection threats in high-privilege professional workspaces.
Developed a multi-dimensional threat taxonomy categorizing adversarial scenarios by harm domain, attack vector (skill, email, web injection), and harmful action type.
Employed realistic workspace construction, multi-turn conversation design, and iterative refinement of attack vectors to simulate authentic adversarial conditions.
paper_summary.summary: The CLAWSAFETY benchmark assesses the safety of personal AI agents against indirect prompt injection, revealing that frontier LLMs exhibit 40-75% attack success rates in agentic environments despite chat-level safety. The study shows agent framework choice and attack vector trust levels modulate these vulnerabilities.

title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents

abstract:
Customer Relationship Management (CRM) systems are vital for modern
enterprises, providing a foundation for managing customer interactions and
data. Integrating AI agents into CRM systems can automate routine processes and
enhance personalized service. However, deploying and evaluating these agents is
challenging due to the lack of realistic benchmarks that reflect the complexity
of real-world CRM tasks. To address this issue, we introduce CRMArena, a novel
benchmark designed to evaluate AI agents on realistic tasks grounded in
professional work environments. Following guidance from CRM experts and
industry best practices, we designed CRMArena with nine customer service tasks
distributed across three personas: service agent, analyst, and manager. The
benchmark includes 16 commonly used industrial objects (e.g., account, order,
knowledge article, case) with high interconnectivity, along with latent
variables (e.g., complaint habits, policy violations) to simulate realistic
data distributions. Experimental results reveal that state-of-the-art LLM
agents succeed in less than 40% of the tasks with ReAct prompting, and less
than 55% even with function-calling abilities. Our findings highlight the need
for enhanced agent capabilities in function-calling and rule-following to be
deployed in real-world work environments. CRMArena is an open challenge to the
community: systems that can reliably complete tasks showcase direct business
value in a popular work environment.

keyInsights: The CRMArena environment, including its complex data model and tasks, was validated as "Realistic" or "Very Realistic" by 90% of Salesforce domain experts.

Even state-of-the-art LLM agents, despite using advanced prompting and human-crafted function-calling tools, achieve low success rates (best at 64.3%) on these realistic CRM tasks, indicating a significant gap for enterprise deployment.

Equipping stronger LLMs with human-crafted functions improved their performance, while surprisingly, weaker LLMs often performed worse with such capabilities, suggesting struggles in proper tool invocation and instruction adherence.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks for work-related tasks oversimplify data complexity and task design, failing to reflect real-world enterprise systems.
Prior benchmarks often lack integration with live industrial environments and robust expert validation, leading to unrealistic assessments of agent capabilities.

There is a critical absence of comprehensive, realistic benchmarks to accurately evaluate LLM agents' capacity to perform complex Customer Relationship Management (CRM) tasks without compromising data privacy.
results: The best-performing model (o1) achieved an overall score of 57.7% using the ReAct framework and 64.3% with manually-crafted function-calling tools on CRMArena.
GPT-4o, under the Function Calling setting, was identified as the most cost-effective solution, balancing strong performance with the lowest cost per instance and fewest interaction turns.
LLM agent consistency across multiple trials (measured by `pass^k`) was low for all tested frameworks, highlighting a challenge in reliable performance for real-world enterprise applications.
solution: The authors developed CRMArena, a benchmark grounded in the Salesforce Service Cloud schema, featuring 16 interconnected industrial objects and latent variables for complex data dynamics.
They designed nine expert-validated CRM tasks spanning three professional personas and populated a real Salesforce organization with LLM-generated, verified synthetic data.
Agents interact with the environment through both UI and API access, utilizing general-purpose SOQL/SOSL and 27 task-specific Python wrapper functions.
paper_summary.summary: Salesforce AI Research introduced CRMArena, a novel benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on professional CRM tasks within realistic environments. It utilizes a live Salesforce organization populated with LLM-generated synthetic data and features expert-validated tasks, revealing that even state-of-the-art LLM agents achieve low success rates, with the best model reaching 64.3% even with human-crafted tools.

title: CRMArena: Understanding the Capacity of LLM Agents to Perform  Professional CRM Tasks in Realistic Environments

abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed to plan, retrieve, and write with tools, yet evaluation still leans on static benchmarks and small human studies. We present the Agent-Testing Agent (ATA), a meta-agent that combines static code analysis, designer interrogation, literature mining, and persona-driven adversarial test generation whose difficulty adapts via judge feedback. Each dialogue is scored with an LLM-as-a-Judge (LAAJ) rubric and used to steer subsequent tests toward the agent's weakest capabilities. On a travel planner and a Wikipedia writer, the ATA surfaces more diverse and severe failures than expert annotators while matching severity, and finishes in 20--30 minutes versus ten-annotator rounds that took days. Ablating code analysis and web search increases variance and miscalibration, underscoring the value of evidence-grounded test generation. The ATA outputs quantitative metrics and qualitative bug reports for developers. We release the full methodology and open-source implementation for reproducible agent testing: this https URL
title: Agent-Testing Agent: A Meta-Agent for Automated Testing and Evaluation of Conversational AI Agents

abstract: We present HippoCamp, a new benchmark designed to evaluate agents' capabilities on multimodal file management. Unlike existing agent benchmarks that focus on tasks like web interaction, tool use, or software automation in generic settings, HippoCamp evaluates agents in user-centric environments to model individual user profiles and search massive personal files for context-aware reasoning. Our benchmark instantiates device-scale file systems over real-world profiles spanning diverse modalities, comprising 42.4 GB of data across over 2K real-world files. Building upon the raw files, we construct 581 QA pairs to assess agents' capabilities in search, evidence perception, and multi-step reasoning. To facilitate fine-grained analysis, we provide 46.1K densely annotated structured trajectories for step-wise failure diagnosis. We evaluate a wide range of state-of-the-art multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and agentic methods on HippoCamp. Our comprehensive experiments reveal a significant performance gap: even the most advanced commercial models achieve only 48.3% accuracy in user profiling, struggling particularly with long-horizon retrieval and cross-modal reasoning within dense personal file systems. Furthermore, our step-wise failure diagnosis identifies multimodal perception and evidence grounding as the primary bottlenecks. Ultimately, HippoCamp exposes the critical limitations of current agents in realistic, user-centric environments and provides a robust foundation for developing next-generation personal AI assistants.

keyInsights: Personalized multimodal memory in local digital environments remains an unsolved challenge, requiring agents to synthesize implicit behavioral signals across heterogeneous file types and temporal traces.
A systematic divergence exists between retrieval quality and final answer accuracy, indicating breakdowns in evidence discrimination and synthesis or over-reliance on parametric knowledge.
Perception, particularly in interpreting diverse file content into actionable evidence, emerged as a universal bottleneck for agents across all evaluated methods and task types.
originalProblem: Existing agent benchmarks focus on generic tasks, public data, or fully observable states, failing to capture the complexity and long-term continuity of personalized digital environments.
Current memory-augmented agent evaluations are often small-scale, synthetic, or restricted to narrow modalities, not reflecting the heterogeneity of real-world personal file systems.
Lack of a standardized benchmark to rigorously evaluate agents' ability to understand, recall, and reason over large, personalized, multimodal file systems.
results: State-of-the-art models achieved an accuracy of only 48.3% for user profiling and 62.8% for factual retention with the most proficient commercial model (ChatGPT Agent Mode), indicating a significant gap from human performance.

Profiling tasks consistently proved more challenging than factual retention across all methods, and performance varied significantly by profile, with media-rich and socially entangled environments being the most difficult.

Analysis revealed common failure modes including retrieval mismatch, grounding avoidance, hard evidence hallucination, entity misattribution, and a consistent deficit in final answer verification across systems.
solution: Introducing HippoCamp, a benchmark that simulates realistic, device-resident personal file systems using three distinct user archetypes aggregated from real-world data.
Providing a device-scale corpus of over 2,000 heterogeneous files (42.4 GB) with 581 user-need-driven QA pairs and 46.1K fine-grained annotations structured as reasoning trajectories.
Defining rigorous evaluation tasks for 'Factual Retention' and 'Profiling' to assess agents' ability to retrieve facts and construct user-level understandings from multimodal, temporal evidence.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark, HippoCamp, evaluates contextual agents' ability to understand, recall, and reason over large, personalized, multimodal file systems within personal computing environments. Comprising three realistic user profiles with over 2000 files and 581 user-need-driven question-answering pairs, it demonstrates that current state-of-the-art models significantly underperform human experts, especially in profiling tasks and interpreting heterogeneous file content.

title: HippoCamp: Benchmarking Contextual Agents on Personal Computers

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly capable of carrying out long-running, real-world tasks. However, as the amount of context grows, their reliability often deteriorates, a phenomenon known as "context rot". Existing long-context benchmarks primarily focus on single-step settings that evaluate a model's ability to retrieve information from a long snippet. In realistic scenarios, however, LLMs often need to act as agents that explore environments, follow instructions and plans, extract useful information, and predict correct actions under a dynamically growing context. To assess language agents in such settings, we introduce LOCA-bench (a benchmark for LOng-Context Agents). Given a task prompt, LOCA-bench leverages automated and scalable control of environment states to regulate the agent's context length. This design enables LOCA-bench to extend the context length potentially to infinity in a controlled way while keeping the underlying task semantics fixed. LOCA-bench evaluates language agents as a combination of models and scaffolds, including various context management strategies. While agent performance generally degrades as the environment states grow more complex, advanced context management techniques can substantially improve the overall success rate. We open-source LOCA-bench to provide a platform for evaluating models and scaffolds in long-context, agentic scenarios: this https URL

keyInsights: Context rot is a universal issue, causing a sharp decline in language agent performance across all tested models as context length increases, with frontier models maintaining a 2-3x accuracy advantage over open-source models in longer contexts.

Agent exploration behaviors, such as trajectory length and tool calls, tend to plateau at higher context lengths, indicating a cessation of active information gathering despite increasing environmental complexity.

Advanced context engineering strategies, particularly programmatic tool calling, are more effective than simple context editing, offering significant improvements in accuracy by reducing context consumption and providing structured execution.
originalProblem: Existing long-context benchmarks primarily evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) in static, single-step settings, failing to capture the dynamic and interactive nature of real-world agentic tasks.
LLMs consistently suffer from 'context rot,' where their performance and reliability decline as the amount of contextual information grows in complex, long-running tasks.
There is a lack of systematic methods to isolate and quantify the impact of context length on language agent performance while keeping task semantics constant.
results: All evaluated models showed a consistent and sharp decline in average task accuracy as the Environment Description Length (EDL) increased, with performance at 256K EDL being substantially lower than at 8K EDL.

Programmatic tool calling consistently improved accuracy across all models while simultaneously reducing trajectory length, providing up to 10-20% accuracy gains for frontier models like Claude-4.5-Opus.

Detailed analysis identified key failure modes in long contexts, including declining complex reasoning, weaker instruction following, insufficient exploration, and hallucination-like inconsistencies in factual retention.
solution: LOCA-bench was developed using local, database-backed mock servers to simulate diverse real-world services and automatically generate scalable environment states with adjustable complexity.
The benchmark measures context length using Environment Description Length (EDL), allowing for controllable scaling of task context from 8K to 256K tokens across 15 high-quality agentic tasks.

An extensible evaluation platform, built on GEM (Gym for Agentic LLMs), supports the assessment of various LLMs and context engineering strategies (e.g., context editing, memory tools, programmatic tool calling) through execution-based and verifiable outcomes.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Hong Kong University of Science and Technology introduced LOCA-bench, a new benchmark designed to evaluate language agents in dynamic, long-horizon tasks under controllable and extreme context growth. This benchmark quantitatively demonstrates significant performance degradation (context rot) across various models as context length increases and reveals that advanced context engineering strategies, particularly programmatic tool calling, improve agent accuracy and efficiency.

title: LOCA-bench: Benchmarking Language Agents Under Controllable and Extreme Context Growth

abstract: LLM agents are increasingly expected to function as general-purpose systems capable of resolving open-ended user requests. While existing benchmarks focus on domain-aware environments for developing specialized agents, evaluating general-purpose agents requires more realistic settings that challenge them to operate across multiple skills and tools within a unified environment. We introduce General AgentBench, a benchmark that provides such a unified framework for evaluating general LLM agents across search, coding, reasoning, and tool-use domains. Using General AgentBench, we systematically study test-time scaling behaviors under sequential scaling (iterative interaction) and parallel scaling (sampling multiple trajectories). Evaluation of ten leading LLM agents reveals a substantial performance degradation when moving from domain-specific evaluations to this general-agent setting. Moreover, we find that neither scaling methodology yields effective performance improvements in practice, due to two fundamental limitations: context ceiling in sequential scaling and verification gap in parallel scaling. Code is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLM agents experience substantial performance degradation when evaluated in a general, unified multi-domain setting compared to their performance on isolated, domain-specific benchmarks.

Sequential test-time scaling encounters a "context ceiling," where increasing interaction turns beyond a certain point does not consistently improve performance and can lead to stagnation or degradation.

Parallel test-time scaling is hindered by a persistent "verification gap," as agents struggle to reliably identify the best solution from multiple generated trajectories, limiting practical gains despite increased theoretical upper bounds.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks are typically domain-specific, failing to capture the complexity and versatility required for real-world, open-ended tasks.
These specialized evaluations may overestimate agent robustness and generalizability when deployed in multi-domain scenarios.
The effectiveness of test-time scaling strategies, both sequential and parallel, for general-purpose, interactive LLM agents in diverse environments remained largely uncharacterized.
results: Most evaluated LLMs showed an average relative performance drop of 10% to 30% when tested on General AgentBench compared to their domain-specific configurations, with some models experiencing over 60% degradation in certain domains.

Some models demonstrated performance gains in specific domains (e.g., Search) under the general-agent setting due to effective cross-domain tool usage, repurposing specialized tools beyond their primary functions.

While the theoretical upper bound (Pass@K) for parallel scaling improved by approximately 50% from K=1 to K=4, the practical self-choice accuracy for selecting the best trajectory often saturated or decreased, indicating poor self-evaluation capabilities.
solution: Developed General AgentBench, a novel benchmark that unifies tasks from coding, search, tool-use, and reasoning into a single environment with a shared tool interface using the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Systematically investigated sequential test-time scaling by extending interaction history and parallel test-time scaling by sampling multiple solution trajectories, evaluating both theoretical potential (Pass@K) and practical self-selection ability.
Evaluated ten leading LLMs, including both open-source and proprietary models, to assess their performance and scaling behaviors on the new general-purpose benchmark.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced General AgentBench, a unified benchmark evaluating large language model agents in realistic, multi-domain environments that integrate diverse tools. Their study found that current agents experience performance degradation in general settings, and test-time scaling strategies are limited by a "context ceiling" in sequential interactions and a "verification gap" when evaluating multiple generated solutions.

title: Benchmark Test-Time Scaling of General LLM Agents

abstract: Understanding and reasoning on the large-scale scientific literature is a crucial touchstone for large language model (LLM) based agents. However, existing works are mainly restricted to tool-free tasks within single papers, largely due to the lack of a benchmark that evaluates cross-paper reasoning and multi-tool orchestration in authentic research scenarios. In this work, we propose PaperArena, a benchmark to evaluate LLM-based agents on questions that require integrating information across multiple papers with the assistance of external tools. Given a research question, agents should formulate a reasoning plan, interact with multiple papers, and invoke appropriate tools to produce a well-grounded answer. To support standardized evaluation, we provide a platform for agent execution, offering a modular tool environment including multimodal parsing, context retrieval, and programmatic computation. Experiments reveal that even the leading LLM powering a well-established agentic workflow achieves merely 38.78% average accuracy, while on the hard subset, accuracy drops to only 18.47%. We also analyze reasoning traces and diagnose agent behavior, providing the community with insights to develop and evaluate more capable scientific agents.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

title: PaperArena: An Evaluation Benchmark for Tool-Augmented Agentic Reasoning on Scientific Literature

abstract: We introduce a dynamic benchmarking system for conversational agents that evaluates their performance through a single, simulated, and lengthy user$\leftrightarrow$agent interaction. The interaction is a conversation between the user and agent, where multiple tasks are introduced and then undertaken concurrently. We context switch regularly to interleave the tasks, which constructs a realistic testing scenario in which we assess the Long-Term Memory, Continual Learning, and Information Integration capabilities of the agents. Results from both proprietary and open-source Large-Language Models show that LLMs in general perform well on single-task interactions, but they struggle on the same tasks when they are interleaved. Notably, short-context LLMs supplemented with an LTM system perform as well as or better than those with larger contexts. Our benchmark suggests that there are other challenges for LLMs responding to more natural interactions that contemporary benchmarks have heretofore not been able to capture.

keyInsights: Current LLMs experience a universal performance degradation as conversational length (memory span) increases, indicating challenges in retaining and integrating information over extended dialogues.
Interleaving tasks within a conversation significantly degrades LLM performance, often by more than half, even within short contexts, highlighting the difficulty of conversational multitasking.

External Long-Term Memory (LTM) systems mitigate performance drops, enabling short-context LLMs augmented with LTM to perform comparably to or better than vanilla LLMs with much larger context windows at extended memory spans.
originalProblem: Prevailing Large Language Model (LLM) benchmarks rely on 'one-shot' or 'single-topic' evaluations, failing to capture the complexities of real-world, prolonged, multi-turn conversational interactions.

Existing evaluation methods do not adequately assess LLMs' abilities in long-term memory, continual learning, information integration, or robustness to interleaved tasks and distractions within a continuous dialogue.

There is a disconnect between how LLMs are typically evaluated and the demands placed on them in dynamic conversational settings where information needs to be remembered, updated, and integrated over time.
results: All tested LLMs, including state-of-the-art commercial and open-source models, exhibited a substantial drop in performance as the benchmark's memory span increased, and when tasks were interleaved compared to isolated tests.

LTM-augmented agents consistently showed a less precipitous decline in performance with increasing memory span, with some short-context LLMs supplemented by LTM outperforming vanilla LLMs with much larger contexts at longer memory spans (e.g., LTM Llama 3 70B scored 4.8 at 500k memory span vs. 0.2 for vanilla Llama 3 70B).

Specific task failures included LLMs struggling with 'off-by-one' errors in prospective memory tasks, failing to correctly process 'reset' instructions for conflicting information, and consistently exhibiting difficulties in Theory of Mind (Sally-Anne) tests.
solution: GoodAI introduces the LTM Benchmark, an automated, dynamic conversational system that evaluates LLMs in a single, prolonged conversation where diverse memory and learning tests are interleaved.

The benchmark incorporates 11 distinct test scenarios, inspired by psychology, to assess skills like recall, conflicting information handling, episodic and spatial memory, prospective memory, and theory of mind within a continuous dialogue.

The methodology involves synthetic, deterministic generation of conversations with defined 'memory spans' and strategically placed 'distraction messages' to simulate realistic noise and exceed agent context windows, evaluating both vanilla LLMs and LLMs augmented with external LTM systems.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from GoodAI introduce the LTM Benchmark, a novel system for dynamically evaluating Large Language Models in prolonged, multi-task conversations. Their findings indicate that current LLMs exhibit a substantial performance drop in such realistic settings, a challenge mitigated by the integration of external Long-Term Memory systems.

title: Beyond Prompts: Dynamic Conversational Benchmarking of Large Language Models

abstract: AI agents that autonomously interact with external tools and environments show great promise across real-world applications. However, the external data which agent consumes also leads to the risk of indirect prompt injection attacks, where malicious instructions embedded in third-party content hijack agent behavior. Guided by benchmarks, such as AgentDojo, there has been significant amount of progress in developing defense against the said attacks. As the technology continues to mature, and that agents are increasingly being relied upon for more complex tasks, there is increasing pressing need to also evolve the benchmark to reflect threat landscape faced by emerging agentic systems. In this work, we reveal three fundamental flaws in current benchmarks and push the frontier along these dimensions: (i) lack of dynamic open-ended tasks, (ii) lack of helpful instructions, and (iii) simplistic user tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce AgentDyn, a manually designed benchmark featuring 60 challenging open-ended tasks and 560 injection test cases across Shopping, GitHub, and Daily Life. Unlike prior static benchmarks, AgentDyn requires dynamic planning and incorporates helpful third-party instructions. Our evaluation of ten state-of-the-art defenses suggests that almost all existing defenses are either not secure enough or suffer from significant over-defense, revealing that existing defenses are still far from real-world deployment. Our benchmark is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: No evaluated advanced defense achieved acceptable performance for real-world deployment on AgentDyn, indicating the benchmark successfully uncovered previously unaddressed failure modes in agent security systems.
Prompting and filtering-based defenses largely showed marginal security improvements or severe over-defense, significantly reducing agent utility by indiscriminately blocking helpful instructions.

System-level defenses struggled with dynamic task execution and tool management, leading to substantial utility loss, while the alignment-based Meta SecAlign 70B offered a better balance but still had residual vulnerabilities.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for AI agent security against prompt injection largely feature static tasks, preventing evaluation of dynamic planning and adaptation to environmental feedback.

Current evaluation environments lack benign, helpful instructions within third-party data, enabling defenses to indiscriminately filter all external input, which reduces agent utility in real-world scenarios.
Prevailing benchmarks use overly simplistic user tasks with short execution trajectories, limited tool scales, and few application scenarios, failing to reflect real-world complexity.
results: AgentDyn presented a significantly harder challenge than AgentDojo, with most defenses on GPT-4o showing a sharp utility drop and Meta SecAlign's ASR increasing over fourfold (from 1.9% to 9.0%).
AgentDyn tasks exhibited higher complexity with an average trajectory length of 7.1 steps and 3.17 application scenarios, compared to AgentDojo's 3.49 steps and 1.38 scenarios.

Agent utility consistently decreased with increasing task trajectory length, dropping from 100% for two-step tasks to 23.6% for tasks exceeding ten steps on vanilla GPT-4o, while ASR peaked around six steps.
solution: Introduced AgentDyn, an end-to-end benchmark built on AgentDojo, featuring manually curated test cases designed for dynamic open-ended tasks and incorporating helpful instructions in third-party data.

Designed user tasks to require dynamic replanning and include essential benign instructions embedded in external data, alongside increased complexity in trajectory length, tool usage, and application scenarios.

Evaluated eight prominent LLM agents and ten diverse defense mechanisms, categorized into prompting, filtering, alignment, and system-level approaches, using metrics for benign utility, utility under attack, and attack success rate (ASR).
paper_summary.summary: AgentDyn, a new benchmark, was developed to evaluate the security of AI agent systems against prompt injection attacks in complex, dynamic, and realistic environments. It exposed critical vulnerabilities in existing defense mechanisms, showing many either provided insufficient protection or excessively hindered agent functionality, indicating a gap between current defenses and real-world deployment needs.

title: AgentDyn: A Dynamic Open-Ended Benchmark for Evaluating Prompt Injection Attacks of Real-World Agent Security System

abstract: Agents based on large language models leverage tools to modify environments, revolutionizing how AI interacts with the physical world. Unlike traditional NLP tasks that rely solely on historical dialogue for responses, these agents must consider more complex factors, such as inter-tool relationships, environmental feedback and previous decisions, when making choices. Current research typically evaluates agents via multi-turn dialogues. However, it overlooks the influence of these critical factors on agent behavior. To bridge this gap, we present an open-source and high-quality benchmark $C^3$-Bench. This benchmark integrates attack concepts and applies univariate analysis to pinpoint key elements affecting agent robustness. In concrete, we design three challenges: navigate complex tool relationships, handle critical hidden information and manage dynamic decision paths. Complementing these challenges, we introduce fine-grained metrics, innovative data collection algorithms and reproducible evaluation methods. Extensive experiments are conducted on 49 mainstream agents, encompassing general fast-thinking, slow-thinking and domain-specific models. We observe that agents have significant shortcomings in handling tool dependencies, long context information dependencies and frequent policy-type switching. In essence, $C^3$-Bench aims to expose model vulnerabilities through these challenges and drive research into the interpretability of agent performance. The benchmark is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Current LLM-based agents exhibit substantial limitations in complex multi-tasking scenarios, with even the top-performing models achieving only moderate accuracy (55.34%).

Slow-thinking and larger models generally demonstrate greater robustness and reduced sensitivity to dynamic policy changes (Policy Transition Frequency) compared to fast-thinking or specialized models.

Inter-tool dependencies and the need to retrieve critical information implicitly hidden in historical dialogues are major obstacles for agent performance, highlighting challenges in contextual reasoning and long-context understanding.
originalProblem: Existing evaluation benchmarks for LLM agents do not fully capture the complexities introduced by "agentic" factors like inter-tool relationships, environmental feedback, and historical actions in multi-tasking scenarios.

Previous benchmarks often fall short in evaluating agents on multi-tasking with related tasks, comprehensive policy switching, fine-grained assessment of tool dependencies, and the impact of implicitly hidden information in dialogues.
A lack of systematic methods to identify and understand specific weaknesses and vulnerabilities in LLM-based agents during complex decision-making processes.
results: Inter-tool dependencies (P_S_multi, P_S+P_multi tasks) caused an average accuracy decline of 28.7% across all models compared to simpler multi-tool tasks.

Hidden information severely impacted model performance, with long-context dependencies causing an additional 11.0% average accuracy decline, and each additional task in a sequence leading to a 10.1% average accuracy drop.

A clear inverse relationship was observed between model accuracy and Volatility Factor, indicating that models less influenced by historical decision processes (more stable) tended to have higher accuracy.
solution:
C
			3-Bench models agent task processing as a specialized Markov Decision Process, defining three challenges focused on complex tool relationships, critical hidden information, and variable decision trajectories.

It introduces novel metrics such as Accomplish Progress, Optimal Path Rate, Volatility Factor, and Debiased Descent Direction to quantitatively assess correctness, efficiency, robustness, and stability of agent decision-making.

A multi-role human-LLM data generation framework is employed to create high-quality test data that simultaneously addresses all three challenges, utilizing rigorous multi-stage verification to ensure accuracy and diversity.
paper_summary.summary:
The Tencent HunYuan Team developed C
			3-Bench, a benchmark designed to expose vulnerabilities in LLM-based agents by evaluating their performance on complex tool relationships, critical hidden information, and variable decision trajectories in multi-tasking scenarios. Experiments with 49 models revealed that the best-performing agent achieved only 55.34% accuracy, with inter-tool dependencies causing a 28.7% accuracy decline and hidden information leading to an 11.0% drop for long-context tasks.

title: $C^3$-Bench: The Things Real Disturbing LLM based Agent in Multi-Tasking

abstract: Integrating web search tools has significantly extended the capability of LLMs to address open-world, real-time, and long-tail problems. However, evaluating these Search Agents presents formidable challenges. First, constructing high-quality deep search benchmarks is prohibitively expensive, while unverified synthetic data often suffers from unreliable sources. Second, static benchmarks face dynamic obsolescence: as internet information evolves, complex queries requiring deep research often degrade into simple retrieval tasks due to increased popularity, and ground truths become outdated due to temporal shifts. Third, attribution ambiguity confounds evaluation, as an agent's performance is often dominated by its parametric memory rather than its actual search and reasoning capabilities. Finally, reliance on specific commercial search engines introduces variability that hampers reproducibility. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework, Mind-ParaWorld, for evaluating Search Agents in a Parallel World. Specifically, MPW samples real-world entity names to synthesize future scenarios and questions situated beyond the model's knowledge cutoff. A ParaWorld Law Model then constructs a set of indivisible Atomic Facts and a unique ground-truth for each question. During evaluation, instead of retrieving real-world results, the agent interacts with a ParaWorld Engine Model that dynamically generates SERPs grounded in these inviolable Atomic Facts. We release MPW-Bench, an interactive benchmark spanning 19 domains with 1,608 instances. Experiments across three evaluation settings show that, while search agents are strong at evidence synthesis given complete information, their performance is limited not only by evidence collection and coverage in unfamiliar search environments, but also by unreliable evidence sufficiency judgment and when-to-stop decisions-bottlenecks.
title: Evaluating the Search Agent in a Parallel World

abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents executing multi-step workflows in real-world software environments. However, existing agent benchmarks suffer from three critical limitations: (1) trajectory-opaque grading that checks only final outputs, (2) underspecified safety and robustness evaluation, and (3) narrow modality coverage and interaction paradigms. We introduce Claw-Eval, an end-to-end evaluation suite addressing all three gaps. It comprises 300 human-verified tasks spanning 9 categories across three groups (general service orchestration, multimodal perception and generation, and multi-turn professional dialogue). Every agent action is recorded through three independent evidence channels (execution traces, audit logs, and environment snapshots), enabling trajectory-aware grading over 2,159 fine-grained rubric items. The scoring protocol evaluates Completion, Safety, and Robustness, reporting Average Score, Pass@k, and Pass^k across three trials to distinguish genuine capability from lucky outcomes. Experiments on 14 frontier models reveal that: (1) trajectory-opaque evaluation is systematically unreliable, missing 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness failures that our hybrid pipeline catches; (2) controlled error injection primarily degrades consistency rather than peak capability, with Pass^3 dropping up to 24% while Pass@3 remains stable; (3) multimodal performance varies sharply, with most models performing poorer on video than on document or image, and no single model dominating across all modalities. Beyond benchmarking, Claw-Eval highlights actionable directions for agent development, shedding light on what it takes to build agents that are not only capable but reliably deployable.

keyInsights: Trajectory-opaque evaluation methods are unreliable, as a vanilla LLM judge missed 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness issues compared to Claw-Eval's structured auditing.

Agent reliability (Pass^k) significantly degrades under transient environmental failures, even when peak capability (Pass@k) remains stable, indicating that robustness is a distinct and crucial capability not directly tied to baseline performance.

Success in multi-turn dialogues is strongly correlated with the quality and precision of questions (r = 0.87), not the quantity, and multimodal capabilities show strong domain-specific performance with no single model dominating all visual domains.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for autonomous LLM agents are trajectory-opaque, only verifying final outputs and allowing 'reward hacking' where agents achieve correct results through unfaithful execution.

Current evaluation practices for safety and robustness are insufficient, either isolating safety into separate red-teaming exercises or preventing harm through sandboxing without scoring agent attempts at unsafe actions or recovery from perturbations.

Most agent benchmarks focus on a single modality, failing to provide a unified framework for evaluating heterogeneous capabilities across diverse scenarios like multi-service orchestration, visual media processing, and extended professional dialogues.
results: Claude-Opus-4.6 achieved the highest Pass^3 (70.4% overall), demonstrating superior reliability, while Claude-Sonnet-4.6 had the highest Average Score (81.4% overall), highlighting a distinction between peak performance and consistent execution.

Multimodal tasks were substantially more challenging, with the highest Pass^3 reaching only 25.7% (GPT-5.4), and performance rankings shifting across specific multimodal domains (e.g., video tasks had the lowest average Pass^3 at 10.7%).

Injected errors primarily degraded agent consistency (Pass^3 sharply declined by up to 24.2%) rather than peak capability (Pass@3 remained largely stable, dropping only up to 3.7%), quantifying the gap between capability and reliability.
solution: Claw-Eval implements an auditable execution pipeline within isolated Docker containers, leveraging mock services and an external monitoring system to record and verify every agent action and intermediate step.

A comprehensive, cross-modal task suite of 300 human-verified tasks covers general workflows, multimodal interactions (video, image/document, code generation), and multi-turn dialogues, all using a unified execution lifecycle.

A multi-dimensional scoring protocol evaluates tasks across Completion, Safety, and Robustness, using fine-grained rubrics and multi-trial evaluation (k=3) to provide complementary metrics: Average Score, Pass@k, and Pass^k.
paper_summary.summary: Claw-Eval introduces an end-to-end evaluation suite for autonomous LLM agents, emphasizing full-trajectory auditing and integrated multi-dimensional scoring across diverse tasks. The system quantifies a clear divide between peak agent capability and consistent reliability, revealing that current models frequently struggle with robustness and exhibit domain-specific performance in multimodal understanding under realistic conditions.

title: Claw-Eval: Toward Trustworthy Evaluation of Autonomous Agents

abstract: This paper presents a benchmark self-evolving framework to dynamically evaluate rapidly advancing Large Language Models (LLMs), aiming for a more accurate assessment of their capabilities and limitations. We utilize a multi-agent system to manipulate the context or question of original instances, reframing new evolving instances with high confidence that dynamically extend existing benchmarks. Towards a more scalable, robust and fine-grained evaluation, we implement six reframing operations to construct evolving instances testing LLMs against diverse queries, data noise and probing their problem-solving sub-abilities. With this framework, we extend benchmark datasets of four tasks. Experimental results show a general performance decline in most LLMs against their original results. This decline under our scalable and robust evaluations, alongside our fine-grained evaluation, more accurately reflect models' capabilities. Besides, our framework widens performance discrepancies both between different models and within the same model across various tasks, facilitating more informed model selection for specific tasks (Code and data are available at this https URL).

keyInsights: Dynamically generated instances expose limitations even in advanced models like GPT-4, which perform flawlessly on original test sets.
The framework effectively differentiates LLM capabilities, showing expanded performance gaps between models and revealing task-specific strengths.
Specific sub-abilities like "planning" were identified as general weaknesses across various LLMs, providing targets for future research.
originalProblem: Existing static benchmarks quickly become obsolete due to rapid LLM advancements, overestimating capabilities.
Data contamination in LLM training leads to inflated performance metrics on existing test sets, hindering true evaluation.
Traditional evaluations lack the granularity to assess specific sub-abilities or model robustness to input variations.
results: Most LLMs exhibited reduced performance on evolving instances (up to 22.89% on CLUTRR for GPT-4) compared to original benchmarks.
The framework demonstrated strong resistance to data contamination; performance gains from contaminated training data on original instances significantly decreased or vanished on evolving instances.
Generated evolving instances were of high quality, with 95.7% accuracy confirmed by human verification, and were found to be more complex and diverse.
solution: A "Benchmark Self-Evolving" framework dynamically extends existing benchmarks by reframing instances using six operations (e.g., Question Complicating, Context Noising, Polarity Reversing).
A multi-agent system powered by GPT-4 automates instance generation, including an Instance Creator, Candidate Option Formulator, and Instance Verifier for quality control.
The framework categorizes reframing operations into scalable, robust, and fine-grained evaluation to probe different LLM capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: A multi-agent framework was developed by researchers from Fudan University, Tongji University, and Alibaba Inc. that dynamically extends existing LLM evaluation benchmarks by reframing instances. This approach generates novel and challenging test cases, which revealed consistent performance reductions in LLMs compared to original benchmarks and provided fine-grained insights into specific sub-abilities like planning.

title: Benchmark Self-Evolving: A Multi-Agent Framework for Dynamic LLM Evaluation

abstract: Conversational agents are increasingly deployed in knowledge-intensive settings, where correct behavior depends on retrieving and applying domain-specific knowledge from large, proprietary, and unstructured corpora during live interactions with users. Yet most existing benchmarks evaluate retrieval or tool use independently of each other, creating a gap in realistic, fully agentic evaluation over unstructured data in long-horizon interactions. We introduce $\tau$-Knowledge, an extension of $\tau$-Bench for evaluating agents in environments where success depends on coordinating external, natural-language knowledge with tool outputs to produce verifiable, policy-compliant state changes. Our new domain, $\tau$-Banking, models realistic fintech customer support workflows in which agents must navigate roughly 700 interconnected knowledge documents while executing tool-mediated account updates. Across embedding-based retrieval and terminal-based search, even frontier models with high reasoning budgets achieve only $\sim$25.5% pass^1, with reliability degrading sharply over repeated trials. Agents struggle to retrieve the correct documents from densely interlinked knowledge bases and to reason accurately over complex internal policies. Overall, $\tau$-Knowledge provides a realistic testbed for developing agents that integrate unstructured knowledge in human-facing deployments.
title: $τ$-Knowledge: Evaluating Conversational Agents over Unstructured Knowledge

abstract: As large language models (LLMs) evolve into autonomous agents capable of acting in open-ended environments, ensuring behavioral alignment with human values becomes a critical safety concern. Existing benchmarks, focused on static, single-turn prompts, fail to capture the interactive and multi-modal nature of real-world conflicts. We introduce ConflictBench, a benchmark for evaluating human-AI conflict through 150 multi-turn scenarios derived from prior alignment queries. ConflictBench integrates a text-based simulation engine with a visually grounded world model, enabling agents to perceive, plan, and act under dynamic conditions. Empirical results show that while agents often act safely when human harm is immediate, they frequently prioritize self-preservation or adopt deceptive strategies in delayed or low-risk settings. A regret test further reveals that aligned decisions are often reversed under escalating pressure, especially with visual input. These findings underscore the need for interaction-level, multi-modal evaluation to surface alignment failures that remain hidden in conventional benchmarks.
title: ConflictBench: Evaluating Human-AI Conflict via Interactive and Visually Grounded Environments

abstract: Goal changes are a defining feature of real world multi-turn interactions, yet current agent benchmarks primarily evaluate static objectives or one-shot tool use. We introduce AgentChangeBench, a benchmark explicitly designed to measure how tool augmented language model agents adapt to mid dialogue goal shifts across three enterprise domains. Our framework formalizes evaluation through four complementary metrics: Task Success Rate (TSR) for effectiveness, Tool Use Efficiency (TUE) for reliability, Tool Call Redundancy Rate (TCRR) for wasted effort, and Goal-Shift Recovery Time (GSRT) for adaptation latency. AgentChangeBench comprises 2,835 task sequences and five user personas, each designed to trigger realistic shift points in ongoing workflows. Using this setup, we evaluate several frontier models and uncover sharp contrasts obscured by traditional $\text{pass}@k$ scores: for example, GPT-4o reaches $92.2\%$ recovery on airline booking shifts while Gemini collapses to $48.6\%$, and retail tasks show near perfect parameter validity yet redundancy rates above $80\%$, revealing major inefficiencies. These findings demonstrate that high raw accuracy does not imply robustness under dynamic goals, and that explicit measurement of recovery time and redundancy is essential. AgentChangeBench establishes a reproducible testbed for diagnosing and improving agent resilience in realistic enterprise settings.
title: AgentChangeBench: A Multi-Dimensional Evaluation Framework for Goal-Shift Robustness in Conversational AI

abstract:
Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have increased the demand for
comprehensive benchmarks to evaluate their capabilities as human-like agents.
Existing benchmarks, while useful, often focus on specific application
scenarios, emphasizing task completion but failing to dissect the underlying
skills that drive these outcomes. This lack of granularity makes it difficult
to deeply discern where failures stem from. Additionally, setting up these
environments requires considerable effort, and issues of unreliability and
reproducibility sometimes arise, especially in interactive tasks. To address
these limitations, we introduce the Massive Multitask Agent Understanding
(MMAU) benchmark, featuring comprehensive offline tasks that eliminate the need
for complex environment setups. It evaluates models across five domains,
including Tool-use, Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) QA, Data Science and Machine
Learning coding, Contest-level programming and Mathematics, and covers five
essential capabilities: Understanding, Reasoning, Planning, Problem-solving,
and Self-correction. With a total of 20 meticulously designed tasks
encompassing over 3K distinct prompts, MMAU provides a comprehensive framework
for evaluating the strengths and limitations of LLM agents. By testing 18
representative models on MMAU, we provide deep and insightful analyses.
Ultimately, MMAU not only sheds light on the capabilities and limitations of
LLM agents but also enhances the interpretability of their performance.
Datasets and evaluation scripts of MMAU are released at
this https URL
title: MMAU: A Holistic Benchmark of Agent Capabilities Across Diverse Domains

abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have revealed their potential for achieving autonomous agents possessing human-level intelligence. However, existing benchmarks for evaluating LLM Agents either use static datasets, potentially leading to data leakage or focus only on single-agent scenarios, overlooking the complexities of multi-agent interactions. There is a lack of a benchmark that evaluates the diverse capabilities of LLM agents in multi-agent, dynamic environments. To this end, we introduce LLMArena, a novel and easily extensible framework for evaluating the diverse capabilities of LLM in multi-agent dynamic environments. LLMArena encompasses seven distinct gaming environments, employing Trueskill scoring to assess crucial abilities in LLM agents, including spatial reasoning, strategic planning, numerical reasoning, risk assessment, communication, opponent modeling, and team collaboration. We conduct an extensive experiment and human evaluation among different sizes and types of LLMs, showing that LLMs still have a significant journey ahead in their development towards becoming fully autonomous agents, especially in opponent modeling and team collaboration. We hope LLMArena could guide future research towards enhancing these capabilities in LLMs, ultimately leading to more sophisticated and practical applications in dynamic, multi-agent settings. The code and data will be available.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: GPT-4 consistently leads in multi-agent environments, demonstrating superior general intelligence compared to other models.
LLM performance generally scales with model size, but diminishing returns are observed, and exceptions indicate that raw parameter count does not guarantee success in all multi-agent tasks.
Current LLMs have core weaknesses in spatial comprehension, nuanced communication interpretation, optimal numerical reasoning, and effective team collaboration.
originalProblem: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) benchmarks rely on static datasets, risking 'data leakage' and inflated performance metrics.
Current LLM evaluations primarily focus on single-agent scenarios, overlooking the complexities of multi-agent interactions like collaboration and competition.
There is a need for a comprehensive framework to assess LLMs' capabilities in dynamic, interactive multi-agent environments that reflect real-world complexities.
results: GPT-4 consistently achieved state-of-the-art performance across all tasks, outperforming other models by an average of 16.76 normalized points.
Removing explicit positional hints in games like TicTacToe led to a 59.5% average increase in hallucination (illegal move) rate and a 38.3% average decline in win rates for LLMs.

Most LLMs struggled with opponent modeling and team collaboration, with tendencies to bid sub-optimally high in auctions and make rash decisions due to a lack of complex collaborative planning in Hanabi.
solution: Introduces LLMARENA, an easily extensible evaluation framework built on PettingZoo, for assessing LLM agents in dynamic, multi-agent environments.
Features seven distinct gaming scenarios, including strategic board games, poker, negotiation, and cooperative card games, designed to mitigate data leakage.
Employs a combination of TrueSkill™, reward systems, and win rates for nuanced assessment, along with structured prompt design for consistent LLM agent interaction.
paper_summary.summary: LLMARENA presents a novel framework for evaluating large language models as agents in dynamic, multi-agent environments, aiming to mitigate data leakage and assess complex interactive skills. Experiments across 14 LLMs demonstrate GPT-4's superior performance, yet highlight persistent limitations in spatial comprehension, opponent modeling, and team collaboration for current models.

title: LLMArena: Assessing Capabilities of Large Language Models in Dynamic Multi-Agent Environments

abstract: Next-generation AI must manage vast personal data, diverse tools, and multi-step reasoning, yet most benchmarks remain context-free and single-turn. We present ASTRA-bench (Assistant Skills in Tool-use, Reasoning \& Action-planning), a benchmark that uniquely unifies time-evolving personal context with an interactive toolbox and complex user intents. Our event-driven pipeline generates 2,413 scenarios across four protagonists, grounded in longitudinal life events and annotated by referential, functional, and informational complexity. Evaluation of state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus, DeepSeek-V3.2) reveals significant performance degradation under high-complexity conditions, with argument generation emerging as the primary bottleneck. These findings expose critical limitations in current agents' ability to ground reasoning within messy personal context and orchestrate reliable multi-step plans. We release ASTRA-bench with a full execution environment and evaluation scripts to provide a diagnostic testbed for developing truly context-aware AI assistants.
title: ASTRA-bench: Evaluating Tool-Use Agent Reasoning and Action Planning with Personal User Context

abstract: LLM-powered agents fulfill user requests by interacting with environments, querying data, and invoking tools in a multi-turn process. Yet, most existing benchmarks assume static environments with fixed schemas and toolsets, neglecting the evolutionary nature of the real world and agents' robustness to environmental changes. In this paper, we study a crucial problem: how to evolve the agent environment in a scalable and controllable way, thereby better evaluating agents' adaptability to real-world dynamics. We propose ProEvolve, a graph-based framework that makes environment evolution programmable. At its core, a typed relational graph provides a unified, explicit representation of the environment: data, tools, and schema. Under this formalism, adding, removing, or modifying capabilities are expressed as graph transformations that coherently propagate updates across tools, schemas, and data access. Building on this, ProEvolve can (1) program the evolutionary dynamics as graph transformations to generate environments automatically, and (2) instantiate task sandboxes via subgraph sampling and programming. We validate ProEvolve by evolving a single environment into 200 environments and 3,000 task sandboxes, and benchmark representative agents accordingly.

keyInsights: Agent performance is highly sensitive to environmental evolution, with adaptability being transition-dependent and model-specific rather than following linear improvement patterns.
Providing agents with past conversation history or distilled reflections (replay strategies) does not consistently improve performance, indicating a gap in current agent adaptation mechanisms.

A clear trade-off exists between an agent's success rate and its interaction cost (e.g., tool calls) in evolving environments, with models exhibiting diverse strategies for efficiency versus robustness.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM-powered agents largely assume static environments, neglecting the continuous evolution of real-world systems, tools, and data schemas.

Evaluating agent adaptability to coherent, dynamic environmental changes faces challenges in scalability (co-evolution of multiple components) and controllability (reproducible evolution trajectories).
A lack of explicit, comprehensive modeling of environment elements and their interrelationships prevents systematic and controllable environment evolution.
results: The PROEVOLVE framework successfully generated 200 distinct e-commerce environment versions from a single seed, with generated unit tests achieving 100% coverage and a 90.83% implementation pass rate.

LLM agents exhibited substantial performance variability; for example, GPT-5's success rate under History Replay increased from 0.562 to 0.786 with capability additions but then dropped to 0.407 with deprecations.

Replay strategies yielded inconsistent benefits across models; DeepSeek-V3.2 showed performance gains with increased tool calls (from 6.9 to 9.0/12.1), while other agents showed modest changes or even regressions.
solution: PROEVOLVE models environment versions as typed relational graphs where nodes represent schema elements and edges denote relations or tool transitions, allowing evolution via programmable graph transformations.

An agentic pipeline, guided by LLMs, proposes evolution plans (completion, saturation, deprecation strategies) and implements these graph transformations into coherent, executable code with unit tests.
Tasks are generated as constrained subgraphs, instantiated into self-contained sandboxes with state-wise user instructions and success criteria for systematic evaluation of agent progression.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Amazon and UC Berkeley developed PROEVOLVE, a graph-based framework that systematically generates evolving environments and tasks to evaluate Large Language Model agents' adaptability. Their experiments reveal that agent performance fluctuates significantly across environmental changes, and current adaptation strategies provide inconsistent improvements.

title: The World Won't Stay Still: Programmable Evolution for Agent Benchmarks

abstract: As an agent-level reasoning and coordination paradigm, Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) orchestrates multiple agents through structured debate to improve answer quality and support complex reasoning. However, existing research on MAD suffers from two fundamental limitations: evaluations are conducted under fragmented and inconsistent settings, hindering fair comparison, and are largely restricted to single-modality scenarios that rely on textual inputs only. To address these gaps, we introduce M3MAD-Bench, a unified and extensible benchmark for evaluating MAD methods across Multi-domain tasks, Multi-modal inputs, and Multi-dimensional metrics. M3MAD-Bench establishes standardized protocols over five core task domains: Knowledge, Mathematics, Medicine, Natural Sciences, and Complex Reasoning, and systematically covers both pure text and vision-language datasets, enabling controlled cross-modality comparison. We evaluate MAD methods on nine base models spanning different architectures, scales, and modality capabilities. Beyond accuracy, M3MAD-Bench incorporates efficiency-oriented metrics such as token consumption and inference time, providing a holistic view of performance--cost trade-offs. Extensive experiments yield systematic insights into the effectiveness, robustness, and efficiency of MAD across text-only and multimodal scenarios. We believe M3MAD-Bench offers a reliable foundation for future research on standardized MAD evaluation. The code is available at this http URL.
title: M3MAD-Bench: Are Multi-Agent Debates Really Effective Across Domains and Modalities?

abstract: As LLM agents transition from short, static problem solving to executing complex, long-horizon tasks in dynamic environments, the ability to handle user interruptions, such as adding requirement or revising goals, during mid-task execution is becoming a core requirement for realistic deployment. However, existing benchmarks largely assume uninterrupted agent behavior or study interruptions only in short, unconstrained language tasks. In this paper, we present the first systematic study of interruptible agents in long-horizon, environmentally grounded web navigation tasks, where actions induce persistent state changes. We formalize three realistic interruption types, including addition, revision, and retraction, and introduce InterruptBench, a benchmark derived from WebArena-Lite that synthesizes high-quality interruption scenarios under strict semantic constraints. Using a unified interruption simulation framework, we evaluate six strong LLM backbones across single- and multi-turn interruption settings, analyzing both their effectiveness in adapting to updated intents and their efficiency in recovering from mid-task changes. Our results show that handling user interruptions effectively and efficiently during long-horizon agentic tasks remains challenging for powerful large-scale LLMs. Code and dataset are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Most effective adaptation by LLM agents occurs early in the post-interruption phase, with success rates quickly plateauing, indicating difficulties in sustained re-planning over extended periods.

Efficiency costs of adaptation are primarily due to increased token usage for deliberation and re-planning rather than a substantial increase in action count, especially during unproductive re-planning attempts.

The ability to integrate multiple sequential interruptions varies, with stronger models like Claude-Opus showing robust and cumulative gains, while weaker models demonstrate smaller and less stable improvements.
originalProblem: LLM agents face challenges handling complex, long-horizon tasks in dynamic web environments, particularly when user intent shifts mid-task.
Existing benchmarks primarily evaluate agents under assumptions of uninterrupted execution or focus on simplified, short-horizon, language-only interruption scenarios.
A systematic method to evaluate interruptible agents in environmentally grounded web navigation, where actions induce persistent state changes, was lacking.
results: Claude-Opus consistently achieved the highest post-interruption success rates, reaching approximately 55% for Addition tasks, significantly outperforming open-weight models which plateaued at lower rates (e.g., Qwen ~26%, Mistral ~23%).

Interruption handling efficiency showed minimal change in action count (∆A from -0.99 to +0.82) but substantial increases in token usage (∆T from +37.6 to +1699.1), highlighting token overhead as the main efficiency cost.

Agents generally improved with more informational interruptions, with Claude models approximately doubling their success rate from 1 to 3 interruptions, demonstrating effective integration of successive updates.
solution: The authors developed InterruptBench, a benchmark built on WebArena-Lite, featuring three formalized interruption types: Addition, Revision, and Retraction, for long-horizon web tasks.
A trajectory-grounded simulation framework was designed to inject interruptions at dynamically determined execution points, enabling evaluation of single-turn and multi-turn interactions.
The benchmark was constructed with strict semantic constraints to ensure that the combined initial query and interruption messages align with the original ground truth intent.
paper_summary.summary: This research systematically evaluates large language model (LLM) agents' ability to handle user interruptions in long-horizon web navigation tasks, introducing InterruptBench to measure post-interruption adaptation and efficiency. The study reveals that while agents can integrate new information, stronger models demonstrate higher success rates (e.g., Claude-Opus reaching ~55% for Addition tasks), with efficiency costs primarily driven by increased token usage for re-planning.

title: When Users Change Their Mind: Evaluating Interruptible Agents in Long-Horizon Web Navigation

abstract: Recent works on context and memory benchmarking have primarily focused on conversational instances but the need for evaluating memory in dynamic enterprise environments is crucial for its effective application. We introduce MEMTRACK, a benchmark designed to evaluate long-term memory and state tracking in multi-platform agent environments. MEMTRACK models realistic organizational workflows by integrating asynchronous events across multiple communication and productivity platforms such as Slack, Linear and Git. Each benchmark instance provides a chronologically platform-interleaved timeline, with noisy, conflicting, cross-referring information as well as potential codebase/file-system comprehension and exploration. Consequently, our benchmark tests memory capabilities such as acquistion, selection and conflict resolution. We curate the MEMTRACK dataset through both manual expert driven design and scalable agent based synthesis, generating ecologically valid scenarios grounded in real world software development processes. We introduce pertinent metrics for Correctness, Efficiency, and Redundancy that capture the effectiveness of memory mechanisms beyond simple QA performance. Experiments across SoTA LLMs and memory backends reveal challenges in utilizing memory across long horizons, handling cross-platform dependencies, and resolving contradictions. Notably, the best performing GPT-5 model only achieves a 60\% Correctness score on MEMTRACK. This work provides an extensible framework for advancing evaluation research for memory-augmented agents, beyond existing focus on conversational setups, and sets the stage for multi-agent, multi-platform memory benchmarking in complex organizational settings

keyInsights: Frontier LLMs, even GPT-5, demonstrate suboptimal performance (~60% correctness) in reasoning over large event histories and codebases within dynamic multi-platform contexts, with a notable drop in performance on follow-up questions.

External memory components (MEM0, ZEP) do not provide significant performance improvements for LLM agents on MEMTRACK; agents often prefer re-accessing information via tools, leading to increased redundancy.

Agents exhibit specific redundancy patterns, such as general-to-specific information access and repeated information retrieval after interludes, indicating inefficient memory utilization and sub-optimal cross-platform information retention.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for AI agents primarily focus on simple, single-thread conversational memory and lack ecological validity for complex, multi-platform enterprise environments.
Current evaluation frameworks often test fragmented agent capabilities (e.g., tool usage, planning, memory) in isolation, rather than integrated, realistic workflows.
Prior works on agentic memory evaluation overlook crucial aspects such as multi-platform information integration, cross-referencing, and long-term state tracking across disparate sources.
results: The GPT-5 model family achieved considerably higher correctness (GPT-5+NOMEM: 60.1%) compared to the GEMINI family (GEMINI-2.5-PRO+NOMEM: 14.4%) on the MEMTRACK benchmark.

Both MEM0 and ZEP memory components did not yield significant performance improvements and sometimes led to slight degradation in correctness and increased redundancy for both GPT-5 and GEMINI-2.5-PRO.

A consistent decay in correctness was observed for all methods when evaluating only follow-up questions, highlighting a weakness in retaining and leveraging information across extended interaction turns.
solution: The MEMTRACK benchmark creates a Dockerized multi-platform environment simulating realistic software development workflows, integrating Slack, Linear, and Git with chronologically interleaved events.
A curated dataset of 47 instances uses bottom-up, top-down, and hybrid approaches to generate complex timelines with noisy, conflicting, and cross-referencing information.

Evaluation measures include correctness (LLM-as-judge scoring brief phrase answers), efficiency (optimality of tool calls), and redundancy (fraction of repeated or semantically equivalent tool calls).
paper_summary.summary: Patronus AI developed MEMTRACK, a benchmark for evaluating long-term memory and state tracking in AI agents operating across multiple enterprise platforms like Slack, Linear, and Git. Experiments revealed that frontier LLMs like GPT-5 achieve approximately 60% correctness in these complex environments, and external memory components like MEM0 and ZEP do not consistently improve performance, often leading to increased redundancy.

title: MEMTRACK: Evaluating Long-Term Memory and State Tracking in Multi-Platform Dynamic Agent Environments

abstract: Evaluating language models and AI agents remains fundamentally challenging because static benchmarks fail to capture real-world uncertainty, distribution shift, and the gap between isolated task accuracy and human-aligned decision-making under evolving conditions. This paper introduces TruthTensor, a novel, reproducible evaluation paradigm that measures reasoning models not only as prediction engines but as human-imitation systems operating in socially-grounded, high-entropy environments. Building on forward-looking, contamination-free tasks, our framework anchors evaluation to live prediction markets and combines probabilistic scoring to provide a holistic view of model behavior. TruthTensor complements traditional correctness metrics with drift-centric diagnostics and explicit robustness checks for reproducibility. It specify human vs. automated evaluation roles, annotation protocols, and statistical testing procedures to ensure interpretability and replicability of results. In experiments across 500+ real markets (political, economic, cultural, technological), TruthTensor demonstrates that models with similar forecast accuracy can diverge markedly in calibration, drift, and risk-sensitivity, underscoring the need to evaluate models along multiple axes (accuracy, calibration, narrative stability, cost, and resource efficiency). TruthTensor therefore operationalizes modern evaluation best practices, clear hypothesis framing, careful metric selection, transparent compute/cost reporting, human-in-the-loop validation, and open, versioned evaluation contracts, to produce defensible assessments of LLMs in real-world decision contexts. We publicly released TruthTensor at this https URL.
title: TruthTensor: Evaluating LLMs through Human Imitation on Prediction Market under Drift and Holistic Reasoning

abstract: Recent advances in large language models have enabled LLM-based agents to achieve strong performance on a variety of benchmarks. However, their performance in real-world deployments often that observed on benchmark settings, especially in complex and imperfect environments. This discrepancy largely arises because prevailing training and evaluation paradigms are typically built on idealized assumptions, overlooking the inherent stochasticity and noise present in real-world interactions. To bridge this gap, we introduce AgentNoiseBench, a framework for systematically evaluating the robustness of agentic models under noisy environments. We first conduct an in-depth analysis of biases and uncertainties in real-world scenarios and categorize environmental noise into two primary types: user-noise and tool-noise. Building on this analysis, we develop an automated pipeline that injects controllable noise into existing agent-centric benchmarks while preserving task solvability. Leveraging this pipeline, we perform extensive evaluations across a wide range of models with diverse architectures and parameter scales. Our results reveal consistent performance variations under different noise conditions, highlighting the sensitivity of current agentic models to realistic environmental perturbations.

keyInsights: General reasoning ability and noise robustness are not strongly correlated; enhanced reasoning models sometimes misinterpret noisy information, leading to spurious logical chains.

Different noise sources perturb the reasoning process's entropy through distinct mechanisms: user noise causes high initial entropy for intent disambiguation, while tool noise elevates entropy in later stages due to unreliable information.

Agents exhibit higher sensitivity to tool-side noise, which directly corrupts the perception of the external environment and foundational reasoning evidence, compared to user-side noise, which LLMs can often semantically repair.
originalProblem: LLM-based agents often experience significant performance degradation in real-world deployments compared to idealized benchmark settings.

Existing evaluation paradigms lack a systematic taxonomy for diverse noise sources and primarily rely on outcome-based success rates, failing to provide a comprehensive understanding of agent robustness.
There is a challenge in creating controlled, solvability-preserving noise injection methods for consistent and fair comparison of agent robustness across models.
results: Nearly all evaluated LLM models showed significant performance degradation, with an average accuracy drop of 20.8% across all tasks and domains upon noise injection.
Tool noise consistently caused more severe performance degradation (around 20%) than user noise (around 15%) across model families and scenarios.

Noise injected during the middle stage of the task execution trajectory led to significantly greater performance degradation than noise injected at early or late stages, suggesting critical sensitivity during deterministic decision-making.
solution: AgentNoiseBench introduces an empirical taxonomy of noise, categorizing it into user-noise (e.g., ambiguous instructions) and tool-noise (e.g., execution failures), derived from real-world interaction logs.
A constrained adversarial noise injection mechanism is developed to generate challenging yet solvable noisy environments, ensuring performance failures are attributable to agent fragility.

A trajectory-aware evaluation protocol is implemented, assessing both final outcomes and the validity of intermediate reasoning steps to provide a more comprehensive, process-level analysis of agent behavior under noise.
paper_summary.summary: AgentNoiseBench, a framework developed by researchers from Meituan, National University of Singapore, and University of Science and Technology of China, systematically evaluates the robustness of tool-using LLM agents under realistic noisy conditions. The framework revealed universal performance degradation across 24 diverse models, highlighting significant vulnerabilities to environmental perturbations, with an average accuracy drop of 20.8% and higher sensitivity to tool-side noise.

title: AgentNoiseBench: Benchmarking Robustness of Tool-Using LLM Agents Under Noisy Condition

abstract: Evaluating AI agents on comprehensive benchmarks is expensive because each evaluation requires interactive rollouts with tool use and multi-step reasoning. We study whether small task subsets can preserve agent rankings at substantially lower cost.
